name: Balloon Analogue Risk Task (BART) · PsyToolkit
description: The Balloon Analogue Risk Task (BART) helps psychologists study how people take risks. In the task, you see a virtual balloon on the screen. With each button press, you can inflate the balloon to earn more points. But if you pump it too far, it might explode, and you lose the points for that round. The trick is deciding when to stop and collect your winnings before the balloon bursts.
credits: Source: PsyToolkit — Stoet, G. (2017, 2010). psytoolkit.org. Used by permission for non-commercial research and education.
citations: Lejuez, C. W., Read, J. P., Kahler, C. W., Richards, J. B., Ramsey, S. E., Stuart, G. L., …​ & Brown, R. A. (2002). Evaluation of a behavioral measure of risk taking: the Balloon Analogue Risk Task (BART). Journal of Experimental Psychology: Applied, 8(2), 75. You can find free versions like this one on academia.edu.

name: Corsi task · PsyToolkit
description: The Corsi test or "Corsi block-tapping test" is a short term memory task conceptually similar to the digit span test. It is named after the creator Philip Michael Corsi, who developed this test as part of his doctoral training (PhD, you can download this original work from 1972, see references at bottom of this page).

name: Flanker task · PsyToolkit
description: The flanker task, also known as the Eriksen flanker task, was designed in the early 1970s by Eriksen and Eriksen. Like most of the paradigms in this library, it is popular and there are many variants of the original.
credits: Source: PsyToolkit — Stoet, G. (2017, 2010). psytoolkit.org. Used by permission for non-commercial research and education.
citations: Eriksen, B. A. & Eriksen, C. W. (1974). Effects of noise letters upon identification of a target letter in a non-search task. Perception and Psychophysics, 16, 143-149.

name: Fitts’s Law · PsyToolkit
description: Eye-hand coordination is the important and complex way we respond to objects we see. For example, if you want to grasp a coffee cup in front of you, you (unconsciously!) estimate the distance between your hand and the cup and make a movement. This seemingly trivial task has been studied in depth by psychologists interested in eye-hand coordination and motor control.
credits: Source: PsyToolkit — Stoet, G. (2017, 2010). psytoolkit.org. Used by permission for non-commercial research and education.
citations: Fitts, P. M. (1954). The information capacity of the human motor system in controlling the amplitude of movement. Journal of Experimental Psychology, 47, 381-391.

name: Navon task · PsyToolkit
description: David Navon’s paper about the speed with which people process global and local information is extremely popular (Navon, 1977). The paper has been cited nearly 1,800 times (checked in the beginning of 2016).
credits: Source: PsyToolkit — Stoet, G. (2017, 2010). psytoolkit.org. Used by permission for non-commercial research and education.
citations: Navon, D. (1977). Forest before trees - Precedence of global features in visual-perception. Cognitive Psychology, 9, 353-383.

name: Go/No-go task · PsyToolkit
description: In many experiment paradigms, people’s response speed and accuracy is measured. In some paradigms people’s capacity not to respond is tested. One of these paradigms is known as Go/No-go paradigms (see also Go/No-go on wikipedia). Typically, in some conditions participants have to respond, while in other conditions they are asked not to respond.
credits: Source: PsyToolkit — Stoet, G. (2017, 2010). psytoolkit.org. Used by permission for non-commercial research and education.
citations: Criaud, M. & Boulinguez, P. (2012). Have we been asking the right questions when assessing response inhibition in go/no-go tasks with fMRI? A meta-analysis and critical review. Neuroscience & Biobehavioral Reviews, 37(1), 11-23.

name: Iowa Gambling Task · PsyToolkit
description: The original Iowa Gambling Task studies decision making using a cards. The participant needs to choose one out of four card decks (named A,B,C, and D). The participant can win or loose money with each card.
credits: Source: PsyToolkit — Stoet, G. (2017, 2010). psytoolkit.org. Used by permission for non-commercial research and education.
citations: Bechara, A., Damasio A.R., Damasio H., Anderson S.W. (1994). Insensitivity to future consequences following damage to human prefrontal cortex. Cognition, 50, 7-15.

name: Lexical Decision Task (LDT) · PsyToolkit
description: In a lexical decision task (LDT), a participant needs to make a decision about whether combinations of letters are words or not. For example, when you see the word "GIRL", you respond "yes, this is a real English word", but when you see the letters "XLFFE" you respond "No, this is not a real English word".
credits: Source: PsyToolkit — Stoet, G. (2017, 2010). psytoolkit.org. Used by permission for non-commercial research and education.
citations: Meyer, D. E., & Schvaneveldt, R. W. (1971). Facilitation in recognizing pairs of words: Evidence of a dependence between retrieval operations. Journal of Experimental Psychology, 90, 227-234.

name: Mental rotation task · PsyToolkit
description: Mental rotation is imagining what a stimulus would look like if it would be rotated.
credits: Source: PsyToolkit — Stoet, G. (2017, 2010). psytoolkit.org. Used by permission for non-commercial research and education.
citations: Shephard, R.N. and Metzler, J. (1971). Mental Rotation of Three-Dimensional Objects. Science, 171, 701-703.

name: N-Back task / 2-Back task · PsyToolkit
description: The N-Back task goes back more than half a century, developed in the 1950s by Kirchner, as you can read in more detail on wikipedia.
credits: Source: PsyToolkit — Stoet, G. (2017, 2010). psytoolkit.org. Used by permission for non-commercial research and education.
citations: Kirchner, W. K. (1958). Age differences in short-term retention of rapidly changing information. Journal of Experimental Psychology, 55, 352-358.

name: Spatial orientation / cueing / Posner Task · PsyToolkit
description: Spatial orientation occurs when attention is drawn to a specific location. Attention can be drawn to a location due to the appearance of a stimulus. In the Posner task, this drawing of attention to a location is called cueing.
credits: Source: PsyToolkit — Stoet, G. (2017, 2010). psytoolkit.org. Used by permission for non-commercial research and education.
citations: Chun, M. M. (2000). Contextual cueing of visual attention. Trends in Cognitive Sciences, 4, 170-178.

name: Psychological Refractory Period Paradigm (PRP) · PsyToolkit
description: The Psychological Refractory Period (PRP) is a fascinating and intensively studied phenomenon which goes at least back to the work by Welford (1952) and is strongly associated by the extensive body of work on this topic by famous psychologist Harold Pashler (1994).
credits: Source: PsyToolkit — Stoet, G. (2017, 2010). psytoolkit.org. Used by permission for non-commercial research and education.
citations: Pashler, H. (1994). Dual-task interference in simple tasks: Data and theory. Psychological Bulletin, 116, 220-244.

name: Simon task · PsyToolkit
description: The Simon task and the Simon effect are named after J. R. Simon. Together with his colleague, he first described this effect in 1963. In essence, it shows that people respond faster and more accurately if there is a match between stimulus and response features (e.g., location, when for example stimulus and response are both located on the left side of one’s body).
credits: Source: PsyToolkit — Stoet, G. (2017, 2010). psytoolkit.org. Used by permission for non-commercial research and education.
citations: Simon, J.R. and Wolf, J.D. (1963). Choice reaction times as a function of angular stimulus-response correspondence and age. Ergonomics, 6, 99-105.

name: Stop signal task · PsyToolkit
description: In a stop-signal task, you are asked to respond quickly, except when a stop signal arrives.
credits: Source: PsyToolkit — Stoet, G. (2017, 2010). psytoolkit.org. Used by permission for non-commercial research and education.
citations: Logan, G.D, Cowan, W.B, & Davis, K.A. (1984). On the Ability to Inhibit Simple and Choice Reaction Time Responses: A Model and a Method. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Perception and Performance, 10, 276-291.

name: Stroop task · PsyToolkit
description: The Stroop Task is one of the best known psychological experiments named after John Ridley Stroop. The Stroop phenomenon demonstrates that it is difficult to name the ink color of a color word if there is a mismatch between ink color and word. For example, the word GREEN printed in red ink. The wikipedia web site gives a good description of the effect.
credits: Source: PsyToolkit — Stoet, G. (2017, 2010). psytoolkit.org. Used by permission for non-commercial research and education.
citations: Stroop, J.R. (1935). Studies of interference in serial verbal reactions. Journal of Experimental Psychology, 18, 643-662. Read here on Classics in the history of psychology.

name: Sustained Attention to Response Task (SART) · PsyToolkit
description: Oops! — Pouring cream into a requested black coffee or throwing away the vegetables while keeping their peelings are examples of action slips common in the everyday lives of normal people [3,21]. Such slips tend to happen when attention to task is degraded through such factors as boredom, worry or dividing attention between several tasks simultaneously. There is a considerable normal variation in action in action-error-proneness [3], and brain damage — particularly to the frontal lobes of the brain — increases the likelihood that individuals will stray from intended goals and hence make errors [33,34].
credits: Source: PsyToolkit — Stoet, G. (2017, 2010). psytoolkit.org. Used by permission for non-commercial research and education.
citations: Robertson, I.H., Manly, T., Andrade, J., Baddeley, B.T., Yiend, J. (1997). 'Oops!': performance correlates of everyday attentional failures in traumatic brain injured and normal subjects. Neuropsychologia, 35(6), 747-758.

name: Task switching · PsyToolkit
description: The difficulty of rapidly switching between two different tasks was first reported by Jersild in 1927, but it was not until the mid-1990s that the task-switching paradigm became popular with cognitive psychologists and neuroscientists. The paradigm’s popularity has probably to do with the fact that task-switching paradigms are so surprisingly difficult.
credits: Source: PsyToolkit — Stoet, G. (2017, 2010). psytoolkit.org. Used by permission for non-commercial research and education.
citations: Jersild, A.T. (1927). Mental set and shift. Archives of Psychology, 89, 5–82.

name: Visual search task · PsyToolkit
description: Visual search is about how we search and find objects using our vision. Research on searching for items in a search display goes back to the work of Anne Treisman in the late 1970s. One of the important ideas of searching for features with specific items (e.g., searching for a green round bottle among a large collection of bottles) is that this cannot be done in parallel; hence, the more items there are to search through, the longer it takes.
credits: Source: PsyToolkit — Stoet, G. (2017, 2010). psytoolkit.org. Used by permission for non-commercial research and education.
citations: Treisman, A. (1977). Focussed attention in the perception and retrieval of multidimensional stimuli. Perception and Psychophysics, 9, 40-50.

name: Wisconsin Card Sorting Inspired Task (WCST) · PsyToolkit
description: Card sorting tests have a long tradition in psychology, going back more than a 100 years to the work of Ach. In 1948, Grant and Berg published their now very famous Wisconsin Card Sorting Test. It is a test of cognitive reasoning. Later, in the 1960s, Milner started to use this cognitive test to assess patient’s level of brain damage to the prefrontal cortex.
credits: Source: PsyToolkit — Stoet, G. (2017, 2010). psytoolkit.org. Used by permission for non-commercial research and education.
citations: Berg, E.A. (1948). Journal of Experimental Psychology, 38, 404-411. A simple objective technique for measuring flexibility in thinking. Journal of Experimental Psychology, 39, 15-22.

name: Attentional Blink paradigm · PsyToolkit
description: The Attentional Blink (AB) phenomenon was first described by Raymond, Shapiro and Arnell in 1992.
credits: Source: PsyToolkit — Stoet, G. (2017, 2010). psytoolkit.org. Used by permission for non-commercial research and education.
citations: Duncan, J., Ward, R., & Shapiro, K. (1994). Direct measurement of attentional dwell time in human vision. Nature, 396, 313-315.

name: ABBA reversed-compatibility effect · PsyToolkit
description: Sometimes, a normal spatial compatibility effect can be "reversed". That happens when people respond more quickly in the stimulus-response incompatible condition than in the stimulus-response compatible situation. The ABBA effect, first described by Stoet & Hommel (1999), is an example of this.
credits: Source: PsyToolkit — Stoet, G. (2017, 2010). psytoolkit.org. Used by permission for non-commercial research and education.
citations: Stoet, G. and Hommel, B. (1999). Action planning and the temporal binding of response codes. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Perception and Performance, 25, 1625-1640.

name: Attention Network Test (ANT) · PsyToolkit
description: Instruction: In each trial, participants see a fixation point followed by a cue (although sometimes there is no cue). After the cue, 5 arrows are shown. Participants must respond with a left/right key to the direction of the key. The original paper does not specify which keys, in this version we use A (left) and L (right).
credits: Source: PsyToolkit — Stoet, G. (2017, 2010). psytoolkit.org. Used by permission for non-commercial research and education.
citations: Fan, J., McCandliss, B. D., Sommer, T., Raz, A., & Posner, M. I. (2002). Testing the efficiency and independence of attentional networks. Journal of cognitive neuroscience, 14(3), 340-347.

name: Automated Operation Span (O-Span) Task · PsyToolkit
description: The Automated O-Span task is a well-known test for measuring working memory (Unsworth and colleagues, 2005). It takes about 20 minutes to complete. It is based on the original O-Span test by Turner and Engle (1989). The automated version runs on a computer and calculates the score automatically.
credits: Source: PsyToolkit — Stoet, G. (2017, 2010). psytoolkit.org. Used by permission for non-commercial research and education.
citations: Turner, M. L., & Engle, R. W. (1989). Is working memory capacity task-dependent? Journal of Memory & Language, 28, 127-154.

name: AX Continuous Performance Test (AX-CPT) · PsyToolkit
description: The AX-CPT is a task where people see two letters in a row, first a cue and then a target (or also known as "probe"). The task was originally designed by Rosvold and his colleagues at Yale University.
credits: Source: PsyToolkit — Stoet, G. (2017, 2010). psytoolkit.org. Used by permission for non-commercial research and education.
citations: Rosvold, H. E., Mirsky, A. F., Sarason, I., Bransome Jr, E. D., & Beck, L. H. (1956). A continuous performance test of brain damage. Journal of consulting psychology, 20(5), 343. Link to journal (behind paywall)

name: Backward Corsi task · PsyToolkit
description: The Corsi task is a test of your working memory. Here we only focus on the differences between the regular Corsi task and the backward test (the regular Corsi task can be called a forward memory span test, in analogy to the forward digit span test).

name: Dimensional Change Card Sort · PsyToolkit
description: Executive functions help a person to organise their thoughts. There are slightly different definitions of what executive function is. One well known contempory author on the topic is Sabine Doebel with whom PsyToolkit worked together to create an online version of the Dimensional Change Card Sort task (Doebel, Stucke, & Stoet, 2021). Dr. Doebel is well know for her work on the the study of children’s executive function (Doebel, 2020).
credits: Source: PsyToolkit — Stoet, G. (2017, 2010). psytoolkit.org. Used by permission for non-commercial research and education.

name: Deary-Liewald task · PsyToolkit
description: Ian Deary is a famous professor in psychology, in particular known for his work in differential psychology and general intelligence. Together with members of his Edingburgh (Scotland) research group, he published an article about a four-choice response task.
credits: Source: PsyToolkit — Stoet, G. (2017, 2010). psytoolkit.org. Used by permission for non-commercial research and education.

name: Digit Symbol Substitution Test · PsyToolkit
description: The Digit Symbol Substitution Test (DSST) is a brief timed coding task (typically 90–120 seconds) in which people use a key to pair digits with symbols and produce as many correct matches as possible.

citations: Jaeger, J. (2018). Digit symbol substitution test: the case for sensitivity over specificity in neuropsychological testing. Journal of clinical psychopharmacology, 38(5), 513-519. Read for free

name: Dot probe task · PsyToolkit
description: The dot-probe task is not only popular in cognitive psychology, but also in other areas, such as educational and clinical psychology. In short, this tasks measures how much faster people respond to threatening stimuli compared to neutral stimuli.

name: Endogenous vs exogenous cueing · PsyToolkit
description: People typically respond faster to target stimuli whose location has previously been "cued". However, this effect turns around when the interval between cue and target is over 300 m (Klein, 20000). This latter effect is known as Inhibition Of Return (IOR).
credits: Source: PsyToolkit — Stoet, G. (2017, 2010). psytoolkit.org. Used by permission for non-commercial research and education.

name: Implicit Association Task (IAT) to measure racial discrimination · PsyToolkit
description: The Implicit Association Task (IAT) is a well known and popular reaction time (RT) test in social psychology and in applied settings (e.g., to help people understand their own unconscious bias). The researchers behind the test claim that the IAT measures a person’s bias that he or she may not be aware of.
credits: Source: PsyToolkit — Stoet, G. (2017, 2010). psytoolkit.org. Used by permission for non-commercial research and education.

name: Inhibition Of Return (IOR) · PsyToolkit
description: Inhibition Of Return (IOR) is a phenomenon first described by Michael Posner and Yoav Cohen in 1984.
credits: Source: PsyToolkit — Stoet, G. (2017, 2010). psytoolkit.org. Used by permission for non-commercial research and education.
citations: Abrams, R. A., & Dobkin, R. S. (1994). The gap effect and inhibition of return: Interactive effects on eye movement latencies. Experimental Brain Research, 98, 483-487.

name: Effects of Visual Grouping on attentional capture · PsyToolkit
description: In everyday life, attention is often captured by things that stand out: bright colours, sudden changes, or irrelevant objects. Classic theories suggest that such salient distractors should interfere with what we are trying to do. A recent study by Ma and Abrams (2023) shows something more subtle: When people expect a distractor and learn to ignore it, the distractor can be actively suppressed.
credits: Source: PsyToolkit — Stoet, G. (2017, 2010). psytoolkit.org. Used by permission for non-commercial research and education.
citations: Ma, X. and Abrams, R.A. (2023). Visual Distraction’s “Silver Lining”: Distractor Suppression Boosts Attention to Competing Stimuli. Psychological Science, 34(12), 1336-1349. https://doi.org/10.1177/09567976231201853. Link to publisher

name: Mackworth clock task · PsyToolkit
description: The Mackworth clock test (Mackworth, 1948) assesses people’s vigilance. Vigilance is a state of concentration that helps people to detect specific events when not much is going on.

description: The reverse digit span task is a modification of the regular digit span task. The basic idea of this task is this:

name: Retro-cue · PsyToolkit
description: In this experiment designed by Vanessa Loaiza and colleagues (2024), people were asked to remember several shapes for a short time. Some of the shapes were concrete, like familiar everyday objects, while others were abstract, meaning meaningless scrambled versions of objects. People usually do better with concrete shapes, because they can connect them to long-term memory (for example, you can label a “chair” in your mind, but you can’t easily label a random squiggle).
credits: Source: PsyToolkit — Stoet, G. (2017, 2010). psytoolkit.org. Used by permission for non-commercial research and education.
citations: Loaiza, V. M., Cheung, H. W., & Goldenhaus-Manning, D. T. (2024). What you don’t know can’t hurt you: Retro-cues benefit working memory regardless of prior knowledge in long-term memory. Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 31(3), 1-12. Open access

name: Sustained Attention to Response Task (SART) with response feedback (SART 2) · PsyToolkit
description: The original SART is a measure of sustained attention. It is a popular task on PsyToolkit, but it has its problems. In Professor Stoet’s opinion, the main problem is that there is no visual feedback for the participant when pressing the correct button. In this version that happens.
credits: Source: PsyToolkit — Stoet, G. (2017, 2010). psytoolkit.org. Used by permission for non-commercial research and education.
citations: Robertson, I.H., Manly, T., Andrade, J., Baddeley, B.T., Yiend, J. (1997). 'Oops!': performance correlates of everyday attentional failures in traumatic brain injured and normal subjects. Neuropsychologia, 35(6), 747-758.

name: Stroop task · PsyToolkit
description: The Stroop Task is one of the best known psychological experiments named after John Ridley Stroop. The Stroop phenomenon demonstrates that it is difficult to name the ink color of a color word if there is a mismatch between ink color and word. For example, the word GREEN printed in red ink. The wikipedia web site gives a good description of the effect.
credits: Source: PsyToolkit — Stoet, G. (2017, 2010). psytoolkit.org. Used by permission for non-commercial research and education.
citations: Stroop, J.R. (1935). Studies of interference in serial verbal reactions. Journal of Experimental Psychology, 18, 643-662. Read here on Classics in the history of psychology.

name: Simon task · PsyToolkit
description: The Simon task and the Simon effect are named after J. R. Simon. Together with his colleague, he first described this effect in 1963. In essence, it shows that people respond faster and more accurately if there is a match between stimulus and response features (e.g., location, when for example stimulus and response are both located on the left side of one’s body).
credits: Source: PsyToolkit — Stoet, G. (2017, 2010). psytoolkit.org. Used by permission for non-commercial research and education.
citations: Simon, J.R. and Wolf, J.D. (1963). Choice reaction times as a function of angular stimulus-response correspondence and age. Ergonomics, 6, 99-105.

name: Face perception · Pavlovia
description:
This experiment builds on exp. 2 in Liberman et al. (2014) and is designed to measure serial dependence in face perception.
The experiement begins with a 30 trial training session for participants to get familiarized with two face images, A and B. Following the training session, two face images are sequentially presented in each trial in the main session. The task is to judge which one of the two face images looks more similar to face image A from the training session an submit this as the response. Following the response, a scale slider will appear on the screen and the participants are asked to adjust the slider between 0 and 1 to represent their level of confidence in their decision.
credits: Source: Pavlovia — contributed by @Anette. Hosted at pavlovia.org. See https://gitlab.pavlovia.org/Anette/face-perception.git for licence details.

name: Word-Position-Experiment-german · Pavlovia
description:
Dieses Experiment ist angelehnt an das Experiment von Stroop (1935).
Im Vergleich zur Originalstudie geht es aber nicht um Farbwörter, die in unterschiedlichen Druckfarben präsentiert werden, sondern um Richtungswörter, die an unterschiedlichen Positionen gezeigt werden. 
Teilnehmende müssen dabei mit den Pfeiltasten auf der Tastatur angeben, um welches Richtungswort es sich handelt und dessen Position dabei ignorieren.

Das Experiment nimmt ungefähr 10 Minuten in Anspruch.
credits: Source: Pavlovia — contributed by @Kaikazze. Hosted at pavlovia.org. See https://gitlab.pavlovia.org/Kaikazze/word-position-experiment-german.git for licence details.

name: Digit Span Task · Pavlovia
description: In this task participants are required to memorise and recall number series in order. Participants start out with three 3-digit sequences. This experiment is based on the original digit span experiment by Jacobs (1887).

name: Hearts and Flowers Task  · Pavlovia
description:
This task is a test of working
memory and inhibitory control
The hearts and flowers task (previously called the dots task; Davidson et al., 2006; Diamond et al., 2007) is a hybrid combining elements of Simon and spatial Stroop tasks. For congruent trials, subjects are to obey the rule, “Press on the same side as the stimulus.” For incongruent trials, subjects are to follow the rule, “Press on the side opposite the stimulus.”

name: Type I - SHJ Categorization Task · Pavlovia
description:
A single dimensional psychopy3 version of the classic SHJ (1961) category task where participants are presented with a series of black and white squares and triangles. Images vary according to their colour (black and white), size (large and small), and type (square and triangle).
A single dimensional psychopy3 version of the classic SHJ (1961) category task where participants are presented with a series of black and white squares and triangles. Images vary according to their colour (black and white), size (large and small), and type (square and triangle).

The task phase begins with a target (+) which remains on the screen for one second and follows each feedback routine. After the target is presented, the stimuli appears on the screen for 7 seconds total. These are pulled from a list of 8 images, referenced in SHJ Category List.xlsx (for Single Dimension). If the participant does not respond in 7 seconds, then they are prompted with a text component asking them to respond quicker.

There are 10 blocks of stimuli in this study, which means that participants will go through a total of 80 images. It takes  approximately 5 - 10 minutes to complete.
credits: Source: Pavlovia — contributed by @catlab. Hosted at pavlovia.org. See https://gitlab.pavlovia.org/catlab/type-i-shj-categorization-task.git for licence details.

name: Seli_etal_2016_CREP · Pavlovia
description:
This is code for the replication of Seli et al. (2016), which is part of the Collaborative Replication and Education Projects (CREP). I share this for other to try out and use.

Note that this public experiment only involves 2 blocks of 45 trials for piloting purposes. The code in the routine SetBlockLengths allows you to change this (to 20 if you are replicating Seli et al., 2016).

Also note that I disabled actual submitting of data by Never stopping the routine Debrief. This means you can only exit the experiment by pressing 'Esc' and closing the browser window. My settings on Pavlovia are such that in that case no data is saved. If you use this for your own experimentation, you should in the routine Debrief change the settting of Debrief_mouse: 'End routine on press' to 'valid click'.
credits: Source: Pavlovia — contributed by @DrJoostDessing. Hosted at pavlovia.org. See https://gitlab.pavlovia.org/DrJoostDessing/seli_etal_2016_crep.git for licence details.

name: sensory-adaptation · Pavlovia
description:
A simple demo of audiovisual temporal adaptation. Participants are presented with sets of flash-beep pairs. In the adaptation block participants passively watch the stimuli. In the respond block participants judge if the beep or flash was presented first. 

Sensory adaptation occurs when participants judgements are shifted by a period of training (e.g. first presenting stimuli with an audio-lead lag may later bias participants to respond that they heard the beep first)
credits: Source: Pavlovia — contributed by @epicpsych. Hosted at pavlovia.org. See https://gitlab.pavlovia.org/epicpsych/sensory-adaptation.git for licence details.

name: SART · Pavlovia
description:
Sustained Attention to Response Time Task (SART)

This SART task is modeled on the framework used in The Irish Longitudinal Study on Ageing (TILDA) 

Participants must press a button (mouse or onscreen) in response to a series of digits and withold responses on the number 3) 

Each digit appears for 300ms, with an interval of 800ms between digits. The cycle of digits 1–9 is repeated 23 times, giving a total of 207 trials. The task lasts approximately for 4min

Citations

 Robertson I. H. Manly T. Andrade J. Baddeley B. T. Yiend J . (1997). ‘Oops!’: Performance correlates of everyday attentional failures in traumatic brain injured and normal subjects. Neuropsychologia, 35, 747–758. doi:S0028-3932(97)00015-8 [pii]

Aisling M. O’Halloran, Ciaran Finucane, George M. Savva, Ian H. Robertson, Rose Anne Kenny, Sustained Attention and Frailty in the Older Adult Population, The Journals of Gerontology: Series B, Volume 69, Issue 2, March 2014, Pages 147–156, https://doi.org/10.1093/geronb/gbt009
credits: Source: Pavlovia — contributed by @farazghz. Hosted at pavlovia.org. See https://gitlab.pavlovia.org/farazghz/sart.git for licence details.

name: The Balloon Analogue Risk Task · Pavlovia
description:
Balloon Analogue Risk Task (BART) 
====

The experiment: 
——

This is a simplified (and prettified) version of the BART task by Lejuez et al (2002). Participants have to blow up a balloon that they know will burst at some point. They ‘earn’ rewards for getting the balloons to be larger, but increase the risk of bursting it, in which case they earn no reward for that balloon. The question is, how many times does someone pump each balloon trying to optimise their rewards. 

The measure is designed to quantify individual differences in risk-taking.

Analysing your data:
——

You should filter out data where the balloon burst and measure the number of pumps made for the remaining trials.

Notes:
——

> **WARNING**: This is an advanced demo involving lots of code components

This can be extended to be more similar to the original paper by adding further colours of balloons with different bursting profiles.

name: Digit Span · Pavlovia
description:
Created for Eliza Yujun Ng December 2018

Numbers are presented at a rate of one per second.
The first digit cannot be 0
Following digits can be 0 but cannot be the same as the previous digit.

If the answer is correct the span is increased for the next trial
If the answer is wrong the span is decreased for the next trial

If there are at least two errors at a given span and that represents more than half of the total attempts at that span then the experiment ends, returning a span of one less than the final span attempted.
credits: Source: Pavlovia — contributed by @lammed. Hosted at pavlovia.org. See https://gitlab.pavlovia.org/lammed/digit-span.git for licence details.

name: Public IAT  · Pavlovia
description:
This IAT is availible to the public for use. 

After forking, please review the algorithm for determining IAT scores prior to unveiling the IAT to your participants. IAT scores are calculated in the code component of the Last_Block. 

Note, this is designed to be both intuitive and easy to alter for your own purposes. Use wisely. 

The Algorithm is based of Greenwalk, Nosek, and Banaji (2003).

Those with questions can contact lirivera@wisc.edu
Copyright 2019, Laur Rivera, All rights reserved.
credits: Source: Pavlovia — contributed by @leonardodallacosta. Hosted at pavlovia.org. See https://gitlab.pavlovia.org/leonardodallacosta/public-iat.git for licence details.

name: Emotion Similarity Judgement · Pavlovia
description: In the experiment, we invite you to judge the similarity of 28 emotions two by two using a 9-point Likert scale (1 = most dissimilar, 9 = most similar). These 28 emotions will be presented on the screen simultaneously as words. Still, each time you will only have to judge the similarity of the two emotions with black borders. Once the experiment has begun, make sure you do not leave until it is over unless you choose to withdraw from the experiment.
credits: Source: Pavlovia — contributed by @liming16. Hosted at pavlovia.org. See https://gitlab.pavlovia.org/liming16/emotion-similarity-judgement.git for licence details.

name: 3D Obj Rec - Movie · Pavlovia
description:
You will rate a pair of 3D abstract objects based on how similar they are for you. Using the keyboard keys, you can choose from 1 (not similar at all) to 5 (extremely similar). You will be asked to do this as quickly and as accurately as possible.
	
This task is one (1) in a group of three (3) similarity-rating tasks. The other two (2) will show you the same group of objects, but rendered with different depth cues each.
	
Thank you for your participation and good luck!
credits: Source: Pavlovia — contributed by @lpvolpi. Hosted at pavlovia.org. See https://gitlab.pavlovia.org/lpvolpi/3d-obj-rec-movie.git for licence details.

name: CRT · Pavlovia
description:
Basic Choice Reaction Time (CRT) created based on the paradigm used in The Irish Longitudinal Study on Ageing (TILDA) 

Participants must click on the start button  and are then presented with either the word "YES" or "NO". They must then click either YES or NO to correspond with the word presented onscreen. This allows seperation of the cognitive RT (time taken to start the movement) and the motor RT (time taken to complete the movement)


For citations from TILDA see:

Setti, A., Loughman, J., Savva, G. M., & Kenny, R. (2015). Trail Making Test performance contributes to subjective judgment of visual efficiency in older adults. PeerJ, 3, e1407. https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.1407

Mosca, I., Wright, R.E. Effect of Retirement on Cognition: Evidence From the Irish Marriage Bar. Demography 55, 1317–1341 (2018). https://doi.org/10.1007/s13524-018-0682-7

Cronin et al., (2013) Health and Aging: Development of The Irish Longitudinal Study on Ageing Health Assessment Journal of the American Geriatrics Society https://doi.org/10.1111/jgs.12197
credits: Source: Pavlovia — contributed by @lpxrh6. Hosted at pavlovia.org. See https://gitlab.pavlovia.org/lpxrh6/crt.git for licence details.

name: Digit Span Task · Pavlovia
description: In this task participants are required to memorise and recall number series in order. Participants start out with three 3-digit sequences. If participants correctly recall 2 out of 3 three sequences, they progress to 4-digit sequence trials and so on. If participants respond incorrectly on 2/3 trials the experiment terminantes. This experiment is based on the original digit span experiment by Jacobs (1887).

name: Cambridge Face Memory · jsPsych Demos
credits: Source: jsPsych experiment-demos repository, contributed by the jsPsych community. Hosted at github.com/jspsych/experiment-demos. See https://github.com/jspsych/experiment-demos/tree/main/cambridge_face_memory for licence details.
description: The first level of the CFMT involves showing six target identities (faces, masked faces or cars) from three different viewpoints (right, front, left) for 3 seconds each. Participants are then asked to identify the identity they have previously seen from a choice of three (3-alternative forced choice task). The second level involves simultaneous refresher of the six identities from one (frontal) viewpoint followed by testing from novel viewpoints and lighting conditions. The third (difficult) level is similar to the second level but includes test images with added visual noise. A short practice level precedes the first level.
citations: Duchaine, B., & Nakayama, K. (2006). The Cambridge Face Memory Test: Results for neurologically intact individuals and an investigation of its validity using inverted face stimuli and prosopagnosic participants. Neuropsychologia, 44(4), 576–585.

name: Ebbinghaus · jsPsych Demos
credits: Source: jsPsych experiment-demos repository, contributed by the jsPsych community. Hosted at github.com/jspsych/experiment-demos. See https://github.com/jspsych/experiment-demos/tree/main/ebbinghaus for licence details.
description: Participants see circular discs presented on either side of the fixation point and need to use the arrow keys to identify which disc is bigger. Each disc is surrounded by a set of smaller discs. Reference disc always have the same size and are always surrounded by discs of the same size. Tests discs vary in size and are surrounded by discs that can either have the same size as the surrounding discs used for the reference discs (control condition), or be a different size, typically smaller (test condition). Every combination of test discs location (left vs right), test vs reference condition, and test disc size is presented in random order across the trials. The number of repetitions of each combination can be modified by the user - note that because test disc location is factored into the design but typically not analyzed, the effective number of trials per test disc size will be twice the number of repetitions. Trials are broken into blocks so that participants can take occasional breaks. When the experiment is complete the results are automatically saved as CSV files. Note, no participant ID is included in the name and files will be overwritten upon a subsequent run of the code.

name: Stroop · jsPsych Demos

description: Participants see rectangles of different color and need to indentify the color of the rectangle. name: SART · Pavlovia
description:
Sustained Attention to Response Time Task (SART)

This SART task is modeled on the framework used in The Irish Longitudinal Study on Ageing (TILDA) 

Participants must press a button (mouse or onscreen) in response to a series of digits and withold responses on the number 3) 

Each digit appears for 300ms, with an interval of 800ms between digits. The cycle of digits 1–9 is repeated 23 times, giving a total of 207 trials. The task lasts approximately for 4min

Citations

 Robertson I. H. Manly T. Andrade J. Baddeley B. T. Yiend J . (1997). ‘Oops!’: Performance correlates of everyday attentional failures in traumatic brain injured and normal subjects. Neuropsychologia, 35, 747–758. doi:S0028-3932(97)00015-8 [pii]

Aisling M. O’Halloran, Ciaran Finucane, George M. Savva, Ian H. Robertson, Rose Anne Kenny, Sustained Attention and Frailty in the Older Adult Population, The Journals of Gerontology: Series B, Volume 69, Issue 2, March 2014, Pages 147–156, https://doi.org/10.1093/geronb/gbt009
credits: Source: Pavlovia — contributed by @lpxrh6. Hosted at pavlovia.org. See https://gitlab.pavlovia.org/lpxrh6/sart.git for licence details.

name: sensory-adaptation · Pavlovia
description:
A simple demo of audiovisual temporal adaptation. Participants are presented with sets of flash-beep pairs. In the adaptation block participants passively watch the stimuli. In the respond block participants judge if the beep or flash was presented first. 

Sensory adaptation occurs when participants judgements are shifted by a period of training (e.g. first presenting stimuli with an audio-lead lag may later bias participants to respond that they heard the beep first)

name: Associative Learning into Eating Disorders1  · Pavlovia
description: The aim of this study is to determine whether likeability ratings of novel food images will decrease after these images are paired together with obese, normal, thin body shapes. If you participate, you will be asked to rate a number of novel food stimuli on how much you think you would like them on a Likert scale from -100 (extremely dislike) to +100 (extremely like). You will then be shown these novel food items again, but each one will also be immediately be followed by an image of different body types (obese, normal, thin). An extinction phase will also be conducted where certain food and body image pairings will not be shown, and the food image will be shown without a body image being shown immediately after. Following these conditioning and extinction phases, you will then be asked to rate both the novel food images and body images using the same Likert scale which will show if your likeability rates differ after viewing the food images being paired with different body types.
credits: Source: Pavlovia — contributed by @mayadas2300. Hosted at pavlovia.org. See https://gitlab.pavlovia.org/mayadas2300/associative-learning-into-eating-disorders1.git for licence details.

name: The Balloon Analogue Risk Task · Pavlovia
description:
Balloon Analogue Risk Task (BART) 
====

The experiment: 
——

This is a simplified (and prettified) version of the BART task by Lejuez et al (2002). Participants have to blow up a balloon that they know will burst at some point. They ‘earn’ rewards for getting the balloons to be larger, but increase the risk of bursting it, in which case they earn no reward for that balloon. The question is, how many times does someone pump each balloon trying to optimise their rewards. 

The measure is designed to quantify individual differences in risk-taking.

Analysing your data:
——

You should filter out data where the balloon burst and measure the number of pumps made for the remaining trials.

Notes:
——

> **WARNING**: This is an advanced demo involving lots of code components

This can be extended to be more similar to the original paper by adding further colours of balloons with different bursting profiles.
credits: Source: Pavlovia — contributed by @MegLun123. Hosted at pavlovia.org. See https://gitlab.pavlovia.org/MegLun123/bart.git for licence details.

name: Lexical Design Task · Pavlovia
description:
This is a simple Lexical decision task. 
A fixation cross will be displayed in the centre of the screen. 
Following this 2 text stimuli will then appear, and the participant will have to respond if they are words or non words pressing 
‘w’ if they are both words or ‘n’ if one or more is not a real word. 
This will be repeated 36 times for the main trial.
Reaction time and accuracy will be recorded, and congruent and incongruent pairs will be compared.
If you have any issues please get in touch with me at 19010167@hope.ac.uk
Thank you
Michael Dolan

name: Digit Span Task · Pavlovia
description: In this task participants are required to memorise and recall number series in order. Participants start out with three 3-digit sequences. If participants correctly recall 2 out of 3 three sequences, they progress to 4-digit sequence trials and so on. If participants respond incorrectly on 2/3 trials the experiment terminantes. This experiment is based on the original digit span experiment by Jacobs (1887).

name: TMB Vocabulary · TestMyBrain
description: For each word, pick which of five words is the closest in meaning (or synonym). This test measures long-term verbal memory, crystallized cognitive ability, and verbal reasoning.
credits: Source: TestMyBrain (Germine lab, non-profit research initiative) — testmybrain.org. Test descriptions and demo videos are drawn from the public TMB Cognitive Tests catalog; the live tasks are administered via the TMB Digital Laboratory and the TMB Digital Neuropsychology Toolkit. Contact the TMB team for research access. Used here for non-commercial cataloging only.

name: TMB Word Sentence Association Paradigm · TestMyBrain
description: Given an ambiguous sentence, decide which of two words provides the best interpretation of the sentence. This test measures negative interpretation biases and information processing.
credits: Source: TestMyBrain (Germine lab, non-profit research initiative) — testmybrain.org. Test descriptions and demo videos are drawn from the public TMB Cognitive Tests catalog; the live tasks are administered via the TMB Digital Laboratory and the TMB Digital Neuropsychology Toolkit. Contact the TMB team for research access. Used here for non-commercial cataloging only.

name: Bouba–Kiki Task · Labvanced
description: The Bouba–Kiki Task measures sound–shape correspondence by examining how people associate unfamiliar words with visual forms. It is widely used in cognitive psychology and linguistics research to study perception, language processing, and multisensory integration.
credits: Source: Labvanced (Scicovery GmbH) — see https://www.labvanced.com/content/research/en/tasks/bouba-kiki-task. Template importable into Labvanced Studio (library id 74107). Run is hosted by Labvanced; full study editing requires a Labvanced account.
citations: Passi, A., & Arun, S. P. (2024). The Bouba–Kiki effect is predicted by sound properties but not speech properties. Attention, Perception, & Psychophysics, 86(3), 976-990.

name: Dot Probe Task · Labvanced
description: The Dot Probe Task is a widely used cognitive and affective attention paradigm designed to measure how quickly individuals orient their attention toward emotional or neutral stimuli. It provides fast, reliable insight into attentional biases, particularly in anxiety, mood, and threat-related processing.
credits: Source: Labvanced (Scicovery GmbH) — see https://www.labvanced.com/content/research/en/tasks/dot-probe-task. Template importable into Labvanced Studio (library id 74545). Run is hosted by Labvanced; full study editing requires a Labvanced account.
citations: MacLeod, C., Mathews, A., & Tata, P. (1986). Attentional bias in emotional disorders. Journal of Abnormal Psychology, 95(1), 15–20.

name: Dual Lexical Decision Task (DLDT) · Labvanced
description: The Dual Lexical Decision Task is a fast paced assessment that measures word recognition, reading fluency, and automatic language processing. Participants judge whether presented stimuli are real words or nonwords. This task is widely used in psycholinguistics, cognitive psychology, and language proficiency research.
credits: Source: Labvanced (Scicovery GmbH) — see https://www.labvanced.com/content/research/en/tasks/dual-lexical-decision-task-dldt. Template importable into Labvanced Studio (library id 74678). Run is hosted by Labvanced; full study editing requires a Labvanced account.
citations: Meyer, D. E., & Schvaneveldt, R. W. (1971). Facilitation in recognizing pairs of words: Evidence of a dependence between retrieval operations. Journal of Experimental Psychology, 90(2), 227–234. https://doi.org/10.1037/h0031564

name: Flanker Task · Labvanced
description: The Flanker Task is a widely used cognitive control paradigm that measures selective attention and inhibitory control. Participants must respond to a central target stimulus while ignoring surrounding distracting stimuli that may be congruent or incongruent.
credits: Source: Labvanced (Scicovery GmbH) — see https://www.labvanced.com/content/research/en/tasks/flanker-task. Template importable into Labvanced Studio (library id 77235). Run is hosted by Labvanced; full study editing requires a Labvanced account.
citations: Mulvihill, A., Matthews, N., Chidley, K., Macmahon, S., Armitage, K. L., Sherlock, D., Dux, P. E., & Carroll, A. (2025). The meta-attention knowledge questionnaire (MAKQ): A new instrument for investigating meta-attention in a young adolescent sample. Metacognition and Learning, 20(1).

name: Mental Rotation Task 2D · Labvanced
description: The Mental Rotation Task 2D assesses spatial reasoning by requiring participants to compare rotated two-dimensional (2D) objects. It is widely used in cognitive psychology and neuroscience to study visual transformation, spatial ability, and decision making under rotation.
credits: Source: Labvanced (Scicovery GmbH) — see https://www.labvanced.com/content/research/en/tasks/mental-2D-rotation-task. Template importable into Labvanced Studio (library id 76954). Run is hosted by Labvanced; full study editing requires a Labvanced account.
citations: Xiang, Z., Huang, Y., Luo, G., Ma, H., & Zhang, D. (2021). Decreased event-related desynchronization of mental rotation tasks in young Tibetan immigrants. Frontiers in Human Neuroscience, 15.

name: Mental Rotation Task 3D · Labvanced
description: The Mental Rotation Task 3D is a spatial cognition paradigm used to measure how individuals mentally transform objects in three dimensional space. Participants evaluate rotated stimuli and decide whether they match a reference configuration despite changes in viewpoint.
credits: Source: Labvanced (Scicovery GmbH) — see https://www.labvanced.com/content/research/en/tasks/mental-3D-rotation-task. Template importable into Labvanced Studio (library id 74968). Run is hosted by Labvanced; full study editing requires a Labvanced account.
citations: Dahm, S. F., Muraki, E. J., & Pexman, P. M. (2022). Hand and Foot Selection in Mental Body Rotations Involves Motor-Cognitive Interactions. Brain Sciences, 12(11), 1500.

name: N-back (2-back) Task · Labvanced
description: The N-Back Task is a cognitive paradigm used to assess working memory and continuous updating of information. Participants must monitor a stream of stimuli and decide whether the current stimulus matches one presented a specified number of steps earlier.
credits: Source: Labvanced (Scicovery GmbH) — see https://www.labvanced.com/content/research/en/tasks/nback-2back-task. Template importable into Labvanced Studio (library id 73892). Run is hosted by Labvanced; full study editing requires a Labvanced account.
citations: Kirchner, W. K. (1958). Age differences in short-term retention of rapidly changing information. Journal of experimental psychology, 55(4), 352.

name: Preferential Looking Test · Labvanced
description: The Preferential Looking Test measures visual preference by tracking where participants direct their gaze when presented with competing stimuli. Participants view multiple stimuli simultaneously, and their looking behavior is used to infer attention and choice. It is widely used in developmental and cognitive research to study perception, learning, and attention.
credits: Source: Labvanced (Scicovery GmbH) — see https://www.labvanced.com/content/research/en/tasks/preferential-looking-test. Template importable into Labvanced Studio (library id 73828). Run is hosted by Labvanced; full study editing requires a Labvanced account.
citations: Teller, D. Y. (1979). The forced choice preferential looking technique for use with human infants. Infant Behavior and Development, 2(2), 135–153.

name: Simon Task · Labvanced
description: The Simon Task measures cognitive control by examining how spatial location influences response selection even when it is irrelevant to the task. Participants respond to a non-spatial feature of a stimulus while ignoring its spatial location, allowing researchers to measure interference between stimulus position and stimulus-response mapping. It is widely used in cognitive psychology and neuroscience to study attention, response conflict, and stimulus-response compatibility.
credits: Source: Labvanced (Scicovery GmbH) — see https://www.labvanced.com/content/research/en/tasks/simon-task. Template importable into Labvanced Studio (library id 78396). Run is hosted by Labvanced; full study editing requires a Labvanced account.
citations: Simon JR, Rudell AP. Auditory S-R compatibility: the effect of an irrelevant cue on information processing. J Appl Psychol. 1967 Jun;51(3):300-4.

name: Stroop Color and Word Test (SCWT) · Labvanced
description: The Stroop Color and Word Test is a classic cognitive task used to assess selective attention and cognitive interference. Participants must respond to one stimulus dimension while suppressing an automatic but conflicting response.
credits: Source: Labvanced (Scicovery GmbH) — see https://www.labvanced.com/content/research/en/tasks/stroop-test. Template importable into Labvanced Studio (library id 73868). Run is hosted by Labvanced; full study editing requires a Labvanced account.
citations: Stroop, J. R. (1935). Studies of interference in serial verbal reactions. Journal of Experimental Psychology, 18(6), 643–662.

name: Trail Making Test · Labvanced
description: The Trail Making Test is a neuropsychological assessment that measures visual attention, processing speed, and cognitive flexibility by having individuals quickly connect sequenced numbers and letters. It’s a fast, reliable tool used across healthcare, research, and performance-based settings to reveal cognitive strengths and weaknesses and support informed decision-making.
credits: Source: Labvanced (Scicovery GmbH) — see https://www.labvanced.com/content/research/en/tasks/trail-making-test. Template importable into Labvanced Studio (library id 73813). Run is hosted by Labvanced; full study editing requires a Labvanced account.
citations: Gaudino, E. A., Geisler, M. W., & Squires, N. K. (1995). Construct validity in the Trail Making Test: what makes Part B harder?. Journal of clinical and experimental neuropsychology, 17(4), 529-535. https://doi.org/10.1080/01688639508405143

name: Ultimatum Game · Labvanced
description: The Ultimatum Game measures fairness related decision making by asking participants to accept or reject offers that divide a shared resource. It is widely used in psychology, economics, and neuroscience research to study social preferences and norm enforcement.
credits: Source: Labvanced (Scicovery GmbH) — see https://www.labvanced.com/content/research/en/tasks/ultimatum-game. Template importable into Labvanced Studio (library id 74094). Run is hosted by Labvanced; full study editing requires a Labvanced account.
citations: Güth, W., Schmittberger, R., & Schwarze, B. (1982). An experimental analysis of ultimatum bargaining. Journal of Economic Behavior and Organization, 3(4), 367–388.

name: Visual Search Task · Labvanced
description: The Visual Search Task measures attentional selection by requiring participants to locate a target within a visual array. It is widely used in cognitive psychology and neuroscience to study visual attention, search efficiency, and perceptual decision making.
credits: Source: Labvanced (Scicovery GmbH) — see https://www.labvanced.com/content/research/en/tasks/visual-search-task. Template importable into Labvanced Studio (library id 77767). Run is hosted by Labvanced; full study editing requires a Labvanced account.
citations: Treisman, A. M., & Gelade, G. (1980). A feature-integration theory of attention. Cognitive Psychology, 12(1), 97–136.

name: Wisconsin Card Sorting Test (WCST) · Labvanced
description: The Wisconsin Card Sorting Test (WCST) is a neuropsychological task that evaluates cognitive flexibility, rule shifting, and executive control by having participants match cards according to changing sorting rules. It measures how efficiently a person can learn, adapt, and modify strategies when the environment changes. It is widely used in clinical, research, and applied performance settings.
credits: Source: Labvanced (Scicovery GmbH) — see https://www.labvanced.com/content/research/en/tasks/wcst-wisconsin-card-sorting-test. Template importable into Labvanced Studio (library id 74078). Run is hosted by Labvanced; full study editing requires a Labvanced account.
citations: Berg, E. A. (1948). A simple objective technique for measuring flexibility in thinking. Journal of General Psychology, 39, 15–22. https://doi.org/10.1080/00221309.1948.9918159

name: Attentional Blink · Labvanced Samples
description: In this study, participants watch a quickly changing series of letters and have to decide if the letter X was presented. This study tests attention and visual perception.
credits: Source: Labvanced Sample Studies (Scicovery GmbH) — template "exp_id=2867" contributed by Labvanced. Run is hosted by Labvanced (https://www.labvanced.com/page/library/2867); full study editing requires a Labvanced account.

name: Balloon Analog Risk Task (BART) · Labvanced Samples
description: The Balloon Analogue Risk Task (BART) is a computerized measure of risk-taking in which the participant pumps a virtual balloon to earn rewards. Each pump increases the potential reward but also the cumulative risk that the balloon will pop, resulting in the loss of all earnings for that round.
credits: Source: Labvanced Sample Studies (Scicovery GmbH) — template "exp_id=76066" contributed by a Labvanced user. Run is hosted by Labvanced (https://www.labvanced.com/page/library/76066); full study editing requires a Labvanced account.
citations: Lejuez, C. W., Read, J. P., Kahler, C. W., Richards, J. B., Ramsey, S. E., Stuart, G. L., ... & Brown, R. A. (2002). Evaluation of a behavioral measure of risk taking: the Balloon Analogue Risk Task (BART). Journal of Experimental Psychology: Applied, 8(2), 75.

name: basketball strategy English · Labvanced Samples
description: In this study, your mental rotation skills will be tested in the form of a basketball strategy game. View and memorize a pathway on a basketball court and recreate the path! This is also a good representation of drawing lines using mouse clicks.
credits: Source: Labvanced Sample Studies (Scicovery GmbH) — template "exp_id=39196" contributed by a Labvanced user. Run is hosted by Labvanced (https://www.labvanced.com/page/library/39196); full study editing requires a Labvanced account.

name: Change Blindness Paradigm · Labvanced Samples
description: In this example of the change blindness paradigm, participants will see a screen flashing between 2 images. When you spot the difference between the images, click on it as quickly as possible.
credits: Source: Labvanced Sample Studies (Scicovery GmbH) — template "exp_id=31898" contributed by a Labvanced user. Run is hosted by Labvanced (https://www.labvanced.com/page/library/31898); full study editing requires a Labvanced account.

name: Chatterbox project · Labvanced Samples
description: The Chatterbox project is a reading test for children. It includes a voice recording of the participant while he/she is reading a story. The participant then answers questions about the text they read aloud.
credits: Source: Labvanced Sample Studies (Scicovery GmbH) — template "exp_id=39492" contributed by a Labvanced user. Run is hosted by Labvanced (https://www.labvanced.com/page/library/39492); full study editing requires a Labvanced account.

name: Corsi Block Tapping Test -Forward · Labvanced Samples
description: Blocks are presented on the screen with one flashing at a time to create a sequence. The participant is expected to click the blocks in the order that they flashed. The number of blocks tapped in sequence starts small and increases with each correctly repeated sequence.
credits: Source: Labvanced Sample Studies (Scicovery GmbH) — template "exp_id=74622" contributed by a Labvanced user. Run is hosted by Labvanced (https://www.labvanced.com/page/library/74622); full study editing requires a Labvanced account.
citations: Kessels, R. P., Van Zandvoort, M. J., Postma, A., Kappelle, L. J., & De Haan, E. H. (2000). The Corsi block-tapping task: standardization and normative data. Applied neuropsychology, 7(4), 252-258.

name: Cued Task Switching · Labvanced Samples
description: Participants complete two different tasks that switch frequently. Cues indicate which task participants must complete for each trial, rather than switching for alternate trials. In this example, participants must either respond to the color or the shape of an object.
credits: Source: Labvanced Sample Studies (Scicovery GmbH) — template "exp_id=39449" contributed by a Labvanced user. Run is hosted by Labvanced (https://www.labvanced.com/page/library/39449); full study editing requires a Labvanced account.

name: Deary-Liewald Task · Labvanced Samples
description: In this sample of the Deary-Liewald Task, participants must press the spacebar as fast as possible when they see an "X" appear on the screen. There is an easy condition and a more difficult condition, both of which test reaction time and visual attention.
credits: Source: Labvanced Sample Studies (Scicovery GmbH) — template "exp_id=35147" contributed by Labvanced. Run is hosted by Labvanced (https://www.labvanced.com/page/library/35147); full study editing requires a Labvanced account.

name: Delay Discounting Task (DDT) · Labvanced Samples
description: In this delay discounting task, participants choose between a smaller amount of money today & a larger amount of money in the future. A combination of 12 time intervals & three hypothetical discount rates were used to create 36 trials + 2 catch trials.
credits: Source: Labvanced Sample Studies (Scicovery GmbH) — template "exp_id=72968" contributed by a Labvanced user. Run is hosted by Labvanced (https://www.labvanced.com/page/library/72968); full study editing requires a Labvanced account.
citations: Leverett, S., Garza, C., & Seaman, K. (2022). The effect of delay duration on delay discounting across adulthood. The Journals of Gerontology: Series B, 77(3), 467-471.

name: Digit Span Text Entry - letters · Labvanced Samples
description: This resource is an example of a digit span study that uses letters instead of numbers, showing how you can use arrays to test working memory of participants. In this study, participants see a sequence of letters for a short time and must remember and report the letters in the correct order.
credits: Source: Labvanced Sample Studies (Scicovery GmbH) — template "exp_id=35111" contributed by Labvanced. Run is hosted by Labvanced (https://www.labvanced.com/page/library/35111); full study editing requires a Labvanced account.

name: Digit Span Text Entry - numbers · Labvanced Samples
description: This resource is an example of a digit span study, showing how you can use arrays to test working memory of participants. In this study, participants see a sequence of numbers for a short time and must remember and report the numbers in the correct order.
credits: Source: Labvanced Sample Studies (Scicovery GmbH) — template "exp_id=35004" contributed by Labvanced. Run is hosted by Labvanced (https://www.labvanced.com/page/library/35004); full study editing requires a Labvanced account.

name: Ebbinghaus Illusion · Labvanced Samples
description: In this classic example of the Ebbinghaus Illusion, participants must adjust the size of a circle to match the perceived size of another circle using the arrow keys. This paradigm tests spatial perception and has been a popular phenomenon in psychology for many years.
credits: Source: Labvanced Sample Studies (Scicovery GmbH) — template "exp_id=27226" contributed by Labvanced. Run is hosted by Labvanced (https://www.labvanced.com/page/library/27226); full study editing requires a Labvanced account.

name: Fitts’s Law · Labvanced Samples
description: In this sample Fitts's Law task, participants click a target and move their cursor to click a second target as quickly as possible. This is a test of reaction time.
credits: Source: Labvanced Sample Studies (Scicovery GmbH) — template "exp_id=36171" contributed by Labvanced. Run is hosted by Labvanced (https://www.labvanced.com/page/library/36171); full study editing requires a Labvanced account.

name: Generalised Anxiety Disorder Assessment (GAD-7) · Labvanced Samples
description: The GAD-7 is a 7-item self-report questionnaire designed to screen for generalized anxiety disorder (GAD). It assesses the frequency and severity of anxiety symptoms over the past two weeks. The GAD-7 is a reliable and valid tool for assessing GAD in both clinical and research settings.
credits: Source: Labvanced Sample Studies (Scicovery GmbH) — template "exp_id=65194" contributed by a Labvanced user. Run is hosted by Labvanced (https://www.labvanced.com/page/library/65194); full study editing requires a Labvanced account.
citations: Spitzer, R. L., Kroenke, K., Williams, J. B., & Löwe, B. (2006). A brief measure for assessing generalized anxiety disorder: the GAD-7. Archives of internal medicine, 166(10), 1092-1097.

name: Hebb Learning · Labvanced Samples
description: The Hebb repetition task, an operationalization of long-term sequence learning through repetition, is the focus of renewed interest, as it is taken to provide a laboratory analogue for naturalistic vocabulary acquisition
credits: Source: Labvanced Sample Studies (Scicovery GmbH) — template "exp_id=39239" contributed by a Labvanced user. Run is hosted by Labvanced (https://www.labvanced.com/page/library/39239); full study editing requires a Labvanced account.

name: Hospital Simulator - Doctor · Labvanced Samples
description: In this study, you will pretend to be a doctor making medical decisions. Sometimes you will receive advice from a colleague.
credits: Source: Labvanced Sample Studies (Scicovery GmbH) — template "exp_id=38186" contributed by a Labvanced user. Run is hosted by Labvanced (https://www.labvanced.com/page/library/38186); full study editing requires a Labvanced account.
citations: Recent advances of HCI in decision-making tasks for optimized clinical workflows and precision medicine

name: Hospital Simulator - Patient · Labvanced Samples
description: In this study, you will pretend to be a patient in a hospital. You will receive recommendations from medical professionals and choose who to agree with.
credits: Source: Labvanced Sample Studies (Scicovery GmbH) — template "exp_id=38188" contributed by a Labvanced user. Run is hosted by Labvanced (https://www.labvanced.com/page/library/38188); full study editing requires a Labvanced account.
citations: Recent advances of HCI in decision-making tasks for optimized clinical workflows and precision medicine

name: Implicit Association Task (IAT) · Labvanced Samples
description: The task is very popular among social psychologists, because unlike the frequently used questionnaire methods, this method (seems to) provide an objective measure of people’s social attitudes.
credits: Source: Labvanced Sample Studies (Scicovery GmbH) — template "exp_id=39160" contributed by a Labvanced user. Run is hosted by Labvanced (https://www.labvanced.com/page/library/39160); full study editing requires a Labvanced account.

name: Marketing Choices · Labvanced Samples
description: This study will examine your spending and saving choices when faced with difficult decisions. This is a useful way to gather marketing information!
credits: Source: Labvanced Sample Studies (Scicovery GmbH) — template "exp_id=28934" contributed by Labvanced. Run is hosted by Labvanced (https://www.labvanced.com/page/library/28934); full study editing requires a Labvanced account.

name: Mental Rotation 3D Task · Labvanced Samples
description: In this mental rotation task, participants must examine an image and choose the images that are matching rotations of the first image. This is a test of spatial cognition and reasoning.
credits: Source: Labvanced Sample Studies (Scicovery GmbH) — template "exp_id=39199" contributed by a Labvanced user. Run is hosted by Labvanced (https://www.labvanced.com/page/library/39199); full study editing requires a Labvanced account.

name: Multimodal Stroop Task · Labvanced Samples
description: In this study, you will be asked to respond to the font color of a word instead of the meaning of the word, while ignoring the word that you hear spoken during each trial. Test your ability to ignore distracting information and respond as fast as possible.
credits: Source: Labvanced Sample Studies (Scicovery GmbH) — template "exp_id=27056" contributed by Labvanced. Run is hosted by Labvanced (https://www.labvanced.com/page/library/27056); full study editing requires a Labvanced account.

name: Navon Task · Labvanced Samples
description: In this sample of the Navon Task, participants must focus their attention on individual letters and shapes made up of smaller letters. Test your perception and see if you can correctly identify the presence or absence of two letters in an image.
credits: Source: Labvanced Sample Studies (Scicovery GmbH) — template "exp_id=36082" contributed by Labvanced. Run is hosted by Labvanced (https://www.labvanced.com/page/library/36082); full study editing requires a Labvanced account.

name: Negative priming · Labvanced Samples
description: In this negative priming task, participants will see words and must respond to the color of the text. The difficult piece is that the words are names of colors and their print color might not match the meaning of the word!
credits: Source: Labvanced Sample Studies (Scicovery GmbH) — template "exp_id=39347" contributed by a Labvanced user. Run is hosted by Labvanced (https://www.labvanced.com/page/library/39347); full study editing requires a Labvanced account.

name: Obsessive-Compulsive Inventory - Revised (OCI-R) · Labvanced Samples
description: Obsessive Compulsive Inventory- Revised (OCI-R) is a brief self-report assessment tool used to measure the severity of OCD symptoms in individuals. The OCI-R consists of 18 items rated on a 5-point Likert scale.
credits: Source: Labvanced Sample Studies (Scicovery GmbH) — template "exp_id=62983" contributed by a Labvanced user. Run is hosted by Labvanced (https://www.labvanced.com/page/library/62983); full study editing requires a Labvanced account.
citations: Foa, E. B., Huppert, J. D., Leiberg, S., Langner, R., Kichic, R., Hajcak, G., & Salkovskis, P. M. (2002). The Obsessive-Complusive Inventory: Development and validation of a short version. Psychological Assessment, 14(4), 485–495.

name: Posner Cueing Task · Labvanced Samples
description: In this Posner Cueing Task, the participant will make a response depending on which of two boxes an "X" appears in. A cue will appear as a distraction, which should be ignored. This study is also an excellent example of the Trial System in Labvanced.
credits: Source: Labvanced Sample Studies (Scicovery GmbH) — template "exp_id=37918" contributed by a Labvanced user. Run is hosted by Labvanced (https://www.labvanced.com/page/library/37918); full study editing requires a Labvanced account.

name: Prosodic Awareness Task · Labvanced Samples
description: In this Prosodic Awareness task, participants will test their recognition of syllable stress in written words and emotion in spoken sentences. Prosodic awareness is a skill that improves with age and language development.
credits: Source: Labvanced Sample Studies (Scicovery GmbH) — template "exp_id=39613" contributed by a Labvanced user. Run is hosted by Labvanced (https://www.labvanced.com/page/library/39613); full study editing requires a Labvanced account.
citations: Wade-Woolley, L. (2016). Prosodic and phonemic awareness in children’s reading of long and short words. Reading and Writing, 29, 371-382.

name: public goods game · Labvanced Samples
description: Be the richest! In this multi-user study, 3 participants will play a pubic goods game. The participants will decide how much money to keep and how much to share with the others.
credits: Source: Labvanced Sample Studies (Scicovery GmbH) — template "exp_id=3162" contributed by Labvanced. Run is hosted by Labvanced (https://www.labvanced.com/page/library/3162); full study editing requires a Labvanced account.

name: Reaction Time Test · Labvanced Samples
description: In this study, participants attempt to stop a clock hand exactly at the 8 position on the clock. This study tests your reaction time and inhibition.
credits: Source: Labvanced Sample Studies (Scicovery GmbH) — template "exp_id=3314" contributed by Labvanced. Run is hosted by Labvanced (https://www.labvanced.com/page/library/3314); full study editing requires a Labvanced account.

name: Receptive Vocab Training · Labvanced Samples

credits: Source: Labvanced Sample Studies (Scicovery GmbH) — template "exp_id=39666" contributed by a Labvanced user. Run is hosted by Labvanced (https://www.labvanced.com/page/library/39666); full study editing requires a Labvanced account.

name: Reference Frame Proclivity Test (RFPT) · Labvanced Samples
description: In this RFPT task, participants will see videos of star fields that contain segments of movement. At the end of the video, participants will indicate which arrow points to the starting position of the video. This task tests movement and spatial orientation.
credits: Source: Labvanced Sample Studies (Scicovery GmbH) — template "exp_id=39295" contributed by a Labvanced user. Run is hosted by Labvanced (https://www.labvanced.com/page/library/39295); full study editing requires a Labvanced account.
citations: Goeke, C., Kornpetpanee, S., Köster, M., Fernández-Revelles, A. B., Gramann, K., & König, P. (2015). Cultural background shapes spatial reference frame proclivity. Scientific reports, 5, 11426.

name: RORSCHACH INKBLOT TEST With EYETRACKING · Labvanced Samples
description: This study is an example of the Harrower-Erickson Multiple Choice Rorschach Test. Participants will freely view an inkblot image with eye tracking, then choose the best description of the image from a list of choices.
credits: Source: Labvanced Sample Studies (Scicovery GmbH) — template "exp_id=39764" contributed by a Labvanced user. Run is hosted by Labvanced (https://www.labvanced.com/page/library/39764); full study editing requires a Labvanced account.

name: SART basic · Labvanced Samples
description: In this Sustained Attention Task, participants must quickly press a key to respond to all stimuli except one. This tests reaction time and sustained attention over time.
credits: Source: Labvanced Sample Studies (Scicovery GmbH) — template "exp_id=36592" contributed by Labvanced. Run is hosted by Labvanced (https://www.labvanced.com/page/library/36592); full study editing requires a Labvanced account.

name: short term priming · Labvanced Samples
description: In this priming task, participants will see quickly flashing stimuli and have to respond to the last stimulus that was presented. This experiment tests priming effects, perception, and reaction time.
credits: Source: Labvanced Sample Studies (Scicovery GmbH) — template "exp_id=39141" contributed by a Labvanced user. Run is hosted by Labvanced (https://www.labvanced.com/page/library/39141); full study editing requires a Labvanced account.

name: Simon Task with Letters · Labvanced Samples
description: In this example of the Simon Task, participants must respond to the meaning of the words "left" and "right" irrespective of the word's position on the screen. The design of this task is a great example of how to use the factor tree to randomize trials proportionally.
credits: Source: Labvanced Sample Studies (Scicovery GmbH) — template "exp_id=27229" contributed by Labvanced. Run is hosted by Labvanced (https://www.labvanced.com/page/library/27229); full study editing requires a Labvanced account.

name: The Flourishing Scale (FS) · Labvanced Samples
description: The Flourishing Scale is an 8-item, self-report measure assessing overall well-being. It gauges self-perceived success in areas like relationships, purpose, and optimism. A higher score indicates greater psychological resources and strengths.

name: Attitudes towards Music · Children Helping Science
description: This research aims to develop a new child-friendly inventory of a popular measure of musicality in adolescence and adulthood: the Gold-MSI: Müllensiefen et al., 2014. At this stage, data is being collected using a larger version of the scale which will be used to calibrate the scale. Once calibrated the new scale will be completed by a large sample of 6-11 year olds to i) investigate the psychometric properties of the questionnaire and ii) develop a new framework for understanding the structure of musicality in childhood. The online questionnaire presents statements about attitudes towards music and asks children to decide how much they agree with each of them on a 5-point agreement scale.

name: Como crianças brasileiras interpretam palavras concretas e abstratas? · Children Helping Science
description: Aprender o significado das palavras pode ser uma tarefa muito difícil para crianças nos primeiros anos de vida. No dia a dia, os bebês precisam descobrir o que as palavras que eles ouvem significam. Podemos pensar que a aprendizagem de algumas palavras ocorre por uma associação direta entre a palavra e o objeto a que ela se refere, por exemplo, se o responsável fala "banana" e aponta para a fruta. Porém, palavras mais abstratas como "deliciosa" e "doce" podem não ser tão simples de serem aprendidas pelas crianças por não indicarem diretamente um objeto visível. Neste projeto, investigamos como e quando crianças brasileiras interpretam palavras abstratas em comparação às concretas. Investigar se bebês já compreendem palavras que exigem uma capacidade de pensamento mais abstrato pode nos ajudar a compreender melhor o desenvolvimento do pensamento e da linguagem nessa fase tão importante da vida. Durante o teste, o bebê verá duas imagens lado a lado enquanto ouve uma palavra relacionada a uma delas. Uma das figuras vai mostrar algo bem concreto, como um objeto familiar, e a outra vai representar algo mais abstrato, como uma sensação ou ideia. Em um momento, a criança ouvirá, por exemplo, a palavra "biscoito" e verá a imagem de um biscoito e de um gato. Esperamos que ela olhe para o biscoito, reconhecendo a relação direta entre a palavra e a figura. Depois, ela ouvirá a palavra "sede" e verá a imagem de um copo e de uma bola. Aqui, esperamos que ela olhe para o copo, já que essa imagem se conecta com a ideia de sentir sede, mesmo que a palavra não esteja nomeando diretamente o objeto.

name: Comparing Quantities While Talking · Children Helping Science
description: Proportional reasoning is important to our everyday lives, but people make a lot of mistakes when evaluating ratios, especially when the ratio in question is broken up into parts. This study is looking to explore the causes of this difficulty. In this study, your child will be introduced to a character named Wally the Bear. They will choose which of two glasses of juice they think will taste the same as Wally the Bear’s juice. If your child cannot click on their own, you can enter their response by clicking on their choice. Sometimes, your child will also be asked to say the word “the” while making their choices.

name: Do some of the dogs have a hat? · Children Helping Science
description: There are many ways that language can be used to give quantity information about multiple objects or entities. We can use inexact words like “some,” “a few”, “each,” “all,” “most,” and “a lot,” precise numerical words like “three” and “four,” and phrases like “at least four,” “only three,” and “many of the Xs.” Previous research has shown that preschool- and kindergarten-aged children have yet to master the nuances of these words and phrases. In this study, we are interested in children’s preferred interpretation of these phrases, and how they change in different contexts and as they get older. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. Once the call begins our researcher will introduce your child to our soft-toy “friend,” who will describe pictures of animals with objects, like cats wearing hats or elephants with balloons. We will ask your child to tell our friend whether their descriptions are good or bad.
