name: Help Koli the koala and Octavia the octopus! · Children Helping Science
description: This study investigates what makes natural categories of things and whether the similairty affects curiosity. We are studying how children change their perceptions of similarity and curiosity as they get older. Studying how similarity between concepts will affect the curiosity about them is espicially interesting with children since we hypotheisze that this link relates to the formation of categories, which is potentially different between children and adults (who already have their categories formed). This study has two parts. In the first, your child will help a Koala named Koli decide how similar pairs of items are. your child will see 50 pairs of images. For example, a cat and a traffic light, and rate the how similar are the two things. At the end, your child will help an octopus named Octavia in rating their curiosity. They will see 40 pairs of images and will be asked to rate how curious are they about the relationship between the two things.

name: How did they sound? · Children Helping Science
description: People speak in many different ways - they may use different languages, different words within a language, or have different accents. We are interested in how 3- to 13-year-old children understand language that sounds more familiar or less familiar. We are also curious about how children’s own experiences with language affect whether they understand different kinds of speech, and whom they like. Your child will hear people speaking in different speech styles - for example, using different pronunciations or ways of saying words that might sound familiar or unfamiliar. To test whether your child understood the speech, we will ask your child to repeat what the speaker said. Then, we will ask your child questions about the speaker, such as how easy the speech is to understand and what they think about the speaker. We will also ask you to fill out a survey about your child’s demographics and language exposure.

name: How Does Attachment Shape Memories? · Children Helping Science
description: We are trying to understand how different ways of feeling close to people (called ‘attachment style’) might affect the way teens remember past events (such as birthdays or special trips, called ‘autobiographical memory’) and how this connects to their guardians. This study will take place on Qualtrics, an online survey platform. Clicking on the “Participate” button will take you to the consent video page. Once you and your child have completed the consent process, you will be directed to Qualtrics to begin the guardians' survey. In the guardians' survey, you will answer questions about your memory and provide some basic information (such as your email address for the reward). On the final page of the guardians' survey, you will find a link to the adolescents' survey. By clicking this link, your child will answer questions about mood, attachment and memory.

name: How many of them have bikes? · Children Helping Science
description: We are studying how children learn the meaning of words like some, every, and all, called quantifiers. They are like numbers, but they have their own conditions of use. Children have been found to make certain errors with understanding these words, and we are trying to see if the types of errors are connected. These words are really important for all kinds of schooling: not just mathematics and science, but ordinary directions like recipes. Your child can help us to figure out the learning path to improve education! In this study your child will be introduced to some new creatures made by a wizard. Pictures show what possessions the new creatures have, and a puppy will attempt to describe the pictures using words like all, none, some. Your child will choose smiling or frowning faces to tell us if the puppy is describing the scenes correctly.

name: Join Adventures with drums and balloons! · Children Helping Science
description: The purpose of our study is to examine how children reason about causal roles, particularly in situations that involve mental states, like emotions. We are specifically interested in how intuitions manifest in cases with multiple causal agents, as well as those in which an agent's lack of action leads to the outcome in question. In this study, we are looking at cases involving causal chains; wherein one agent's action affects another agent and that plays a causal role in the outcome. This will help us learn more about how children interpret causes and responsibility in situations where multiple actors are involved. In our study, your child will watch a brief animated video and answer a few questions. The video will introduce characters and describe a situation, and then your child will be asked about which characters are causally responsible for the outcomes.

name: Kire mutat a fiú? · Children Helping Science
description: Cél: Számos kérdés, amelyet a gyermeke hallani fog, az „ő” névmást tartalmazza: ebben a tanulmányban arra vagyunk kíváncsiak, hogy gyermeke hogyan értelmezi ezt a névmást. Néha a gyermekek értelmezése eltérhet a felnőttek értelmezésétől, ezért szeretnénk megérteni, hogy miért van ez így, és hogy hány éves korban tűnnek el ezek a különbségek. Ebben a tanulmányban a gyermeke olyan képeket fog látni, melyek két embert ábrázolnak, akik közül az egyik egy cselekvést végez. Például az egyik kép lehet egy fiú, aki a nagyapjára mutat. Ezután a gyermeke a kép leírását fog hallani, és eldönti, hogy a leírás helyes-e vagy sem.

name: Learning and Teaching Categories! · Children Helping Science
description: Young children encounter many different objects in their day-to-day lives, some of which are explicitly labeled or categorized, and many of which are not. We are interested in understanding how labeled and unlabeled objects inform how children form categories, especially when the labeled objects are not the most representative of their category. This study will also help us understand how children use category examples to teach others about that category. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. On a Zoom call with a researcher, your child will see a series of differently colored and shaped objects and will hear a made-up category label associated with a few of those objects. They may also hear a description of the labeled objects. Your child will then be asked to teach others about the made-up category by choosing the objects they think best represent this category. Because we’re focusing on children hearing about category labels in English, we can only include children who are hearing English at home.

name: Let's Learn About Scientific Systems! · Children Helping Science
description: Learning about new scientific systems can be challenging, as it requires applying what we already know to unfamiliar concepts. This study explores ways to help children better understand scientific systems. Specifically, we are investigating whether providing children with opportunities to categorize scientific systems based on similarities in their underlying causal structures can help them learn about new examples. For example, does teaching children that the water cycle and the life cycle both include a series of events that repeat in a regular pattern help them learn about other scientific cycles? Our findings could play a key role in improving how science is taught to children. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Participate Now!” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. During the call, we will explain different science concepts to your child while showing them pictures of how the system works! We will then ask them questions about what they learned and about which systems work in a similar way.

name: Let's learn how to dax! · Children Helping Science
description: In school, children are often selected into ability-based groups (e.g., based on performance on certain tests) or clubs (e.g., chess). However, gender and racial under-representation may persist within these groups. At what age are children sensitive to under-representation, and do they use it as an indication of their own ability? This work examines the role that under-representation plays in shaping children's perceptions of themselves and others. Your child will learn a new activity called "daxing" (a fun novel visual search task). They will dax four times to try out for the "Daxing Club". Afterwards, your child will answer questions about daxing, what they think of their own daxing ability, and why they think they did or did not get into the club.

name: Let's Listen to a New Language! · Children Helping Science
description: We are interested in how nonnative language exposure influences how infants learn from nonnative languages. At this age without any prior exposure, a nonnative language like German does not boost English-learning infants' cognitive abilities, such as the ability to form an object category. We are testing whether a few minutes of exposure to German will lead infants to learn from German on the object categorization task that follows. This will provide exciting insights into the power of language exposure on nonnative language learning and early cognitive development. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. In this study, you will first fill out a short survey about you and your baby, such as demographics and your child's language experience. Then, your baby will listen to a 3 minute story in German. After that, they will see images of objects (such as dinosaurs and fish) while listening to a phrase in German. We will measure their looking time to see if the German speech helps them to form an object category.

name: ¡Let’s read un cuento! · Children Helping Science
description: We want to understand how bilingual children learn new words and meanings from storybooks. Bilingual storybooks come in different formats—some use one language at a time, others switch between two. Despite these two formats frequently appearing in published books, we do not know which is best for bilingual children’s learning. In the sessions involved in this study, children will see different storybook formats and complete language games. By joining, you’ll help us understand how different story styles can best support bilingual children’s word learning and what language skills they use to learn! This study takes place over 1-3 live video calls with a researcher. During the study, your child will hear bilingual stories about characters who go on adventures with help from their friends. As they get ready, they learn about new tools and how they’re used. After each story, your child will answer a few simple questions and retell the story in their own words. They’ll also play language games in Spanish and English, as well as fun activities with numbers and patterns. When you click “Participate now,” you will be directed to an online calendar to choose the date and time that works best for your first session. Once you schedule your session, we’ll send you a consent form and a Zoom link. This study will include up to three sessions. After you fill out the consent form, we will let you know if you are eligible to complete all three sessions! Each session lasts about an hour and is designed to be interactive and enjoyable for your child!

name: Linking speech sounds to images · Children Helping Science
description: Individuals tend to associate certain speech sounds with certain features. For example, to most people an invented word such as "teepteep" would sound smaller than "gowba". There is evidence that children learn words more successfully when the sounds in a word resemble their meaning. We are interested in finding out why these kinds of words have a learning benefit, and how early in development infants are able to perceive similarities between word and meaning. This study will take place on a video call with a live researcher! Clicking on the “Participate Now!” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you.During this study, your baby will look at images of colorful shapes and listen to a speaker labeling these objects. Occasionally an image of a bus will appear, accompanied by the sound of horns. Your child will not need to do anything other than look at the computer screen. Because this study involves language, to avoid complication, we're only looking for babies who were born in the US and live in households where American English is spoken.

name: Multiple Identities and Flexible Thinking · Children Helping Science
description: The purpose of this study is to explore how parents of children from diverse racial and cultural backgrounds teach their kids about race and culture. Specifically, it will collect data on participants' socialization by means of essentialism (the idea that a group shares fixed and unchanging traits that define them) and colorblindness (the assertion that race should be ignored in order to promote equality) scales, which relate to their responses when their identities are questioned. We want to know how families may prepare their children for experiences related to their identity and background, especially when they come from more than one heritage. The whole online Qualtrics survey can be completed at any time. There are two parts to the survey that are all in the same link--one part for your child and one part for you. After filling out a consent form, parents will report some demographics. Then, the child will participate in several tasks that demonstrate their understanding of social categories, traits, and identity denial. They will be asked to complete a sorting task, a task where they respond to scenarios of identity denial, a switched-at-birth task, and to fill out a questionnaire that asks about how they learn about their race and/or culture. The parent portion includes questionnaires about how they share the following within their household: their cultural background, how fixed social categories may be, and how they may prepare for bias.

name: Pain-related Stigma and Stress in Youth with Chronic Pain · Children Helping Science
description: This study seeks to (1) evaluate the potential association between pain-related stigma and psychological stress in youth with chronic pain and (2) examine how these constructs may relate to overall psychosocial (e.g., symptoms of anxiety, depression) and pain-related (e.g., pain intensity, functional disability) functioning and treatment engagement in these youth. Your child will be asked to answer some brief questionnaires about how they think about pain, their engagement in various activities, their mood/worries, and their day-to-day functioning.

name: Social image among peers · Children Helping Science
description: Children quickly learn that social behavior shapes how others see us. In everyday life, children make decisions about what to say, whom to associate with, and whose behavior to follow, all while considering how these choices might affect their social image. In this study, we are interested in how children think about reputation and social impressions: what kinds of actions make someone seem smart, kind, popular, or embarrassing to be connected with. To capture the range of situations in which reputations are formed, we examine several familiar social contexts. In some stories, children consider how people might speak or act when they want to appear knowledgeable or nice to others. In conversational situations, we explore how children interpret what people choose to share or explain during interactions and what those choices reveal socially. In online and peer-group contexts, we look at how children reason about feeling embarrassed through association and how social connections can influence reputation. Finally, we examine how children think about popularity and imitation. By bringing together these different scenarios, we aim to better understand how children reason about managing impressions and navigating social reputation in their everyday social worlds. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. During the scheduled Zoom session, a researcher will read aloud or show videos of age-appropriate stories to your child. The stories involve children talking with classmates and teachers in familiar situations. For example, in one story, a child is selected to lead a group project and wants people to think she is smart or nice. After each story, your child will be asked what they think about the characters, such as what they think the characters know, what kind of qualities they possess, or what a character might choose to do in a situation.

name: What did the genie do? · Children Helping Science
description: This study explores how children use and understand phrases that refer to multiple entities. Expressions like “all of the cats,” “some of the cats,” “three cats,” and “the cats” all describe more than one cat, but differ in what they communicate—such as whether the cats form a group, whether they’re treated as individuals, and whether the quantity is exact or vague. We’re particularly interested in whether the way multiple entities are grouped—or not grouped—influences the kinds of phrases children prefer. For instance, do children choose different expressions when the cats are presented as a group versus as separate individuals? Since adult language offers multiple ways to describe sets and individuals, we aim to understand what distinctions children are sensitive to as they acquire this aspect of language. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. During a scheduled Zoom call with one of our researchers, your child will watch a series of short videos featuring a genie and his animal friends. In each one, they’ll hear a pre-recorded description of the video with a missing word. Your child will be asked to repeat the full description or say the missing word.

name: Animals Hide and Seek! · Children Helping Science
description: We are interested in how well babies remember which objects they have seen, and whether they remember better if the object (like a particular dog) was given its own name. Babies pay close attention to what we say to them, even before they can produce their own words. This tells us that how we name objects for them can already guide how they think about these objects. Here, we are asking if babies can remember that an object is the same one even after it disappears and then reappears, and whether the way we name the object influences how well they remember it. In this study, you will be asked to fill out a short survey where you will indicate from a list of words which ones your baby understands and says. Then, your baby will watch a short video filled with cute, stuffed animals and hear names for these animals (like "dax"). Then, the animals will hide behind a screen, and your baby will watch as the screen is then raised. We will record how interested your baby is in looking at what animal comes out from behind the screen. Lastly, your child will watch short videos testing their memory of colorful shapes.

name: Apples, Apples....Uh-Oh! · Children Helping Science
description: We’re all part of different kinds of relationships where some people are in charge, like parents with their kids, teachers with students, or bosses with employees. Some leaders are in charge because they are strong and assertive, while others lead because they are smart and helpful. In this study, we want to understand how children think leaders in these different types of roles will share resources, like apples, with others in their group. This helps us learn more about how children understand fairness and leadership, which can help us better understand how they think about people in charge in their everyday lives. You and your child will watch a short story about two leaders. Then, your child will be asked, 'Will the leader cross the river/jump over a trench, or make the follower do it?' and will predict what happens next by clicking on the picture they think completes the story.

name: Aprendiendo Nombres de Juguetes Nuevos · Children Helping Science
description: The goal of this study is to figure out how children who grow up hearing both English and Spanish learn new words when both languages are used in the same sentence. We’re really interested in whether hearing that switch between the two languages affects how much kids pay attention to information that comes later. This project will help us to learn more about how children growing up in bilingual homes are able to learn from both of their languages. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you (and you can choose whether to meet with a researcher in English or in Spanish). During the study, your child will look at pictures of unfamiliar objects and listen to somebody naming them using English and Spanish. We will record your child’s eye movements to see which object they look at when only one is named. Important note: Because we are looking at eye movements, we ask that you join the Zoom meeting from a laptop or desktop computer (not mobile or tablet). We might also ask you to turn lights on or off during the session so that we can better see your child’s eyes.

name: Are there some buttons or many buttons? · Children Helping Science
description: We are investigating whether children can change their expectations about what words a person will use after hearing them speak. This is interesting because children often assume that all people will speak the same way, but this isn't actually true in real life. For example, one person might use "some" to describe proportions up to 30%, but other people would use "some" up to 60%. In previous studies, we have found that children can update their expectations about how a speaker will use words like "some" and "many" after hearing them use those words. We are interested in whether these expectations are specific to the domain they observed or if children generalize across domains. In this study, children hear Mrs. Bear talk about candies, and we are interested in whether this changes their expectations about what she will say about a different domain, namely buttons. In this study, your child will meet Mrs. Bear and hear her describe jars with different proportions of candy and rocks with sentences like "Some of them are candy" and "Many of them are candy." She will do this for items in 10 different jars. Then, your child will see jars with different proportions of buttons and shells and will choose whether they think Mrs. Bear would say, "Some of them are buttons" or "Many of them are buttons." Your child will do this for 19 different jars.

name: Babies' reactions to unfamiliar foods · Children Helping Science
description: In this study, we are interested in learning more about babies’ and toddlers’ reactions to unfamiliar foods (new foods they have not tasted before). Different children react differently to new foods and we would like to learn more about the factors that influence eating behavior in the first two years. This study may increase our understanding of the development of eating and food preferences early in life. We hope that this benefits the scientific community and will help us develop recommendations and best practices for parents as they introduce new foods to their babies and toddlers. This study will take place on two video calls, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time for the first session. At the end of that session, we will schedule a follow-up session for about 1 week later. We will ask you to select unfamiliar foods and offer them to your child in two different videoconference appointments (a different unfamiliar food in each session). Before the first appointment, we will ask you to fill out an online permission form and complete questionnaires. In the first appointment, just do what you would do normally if we were not interacting with you. In the second appointment, we will give you some instructions and see how you and your child respond. You are welcome to pick any foods that are unfamiliar to your child that you feel comfortable offering. We just ask that you select one food to start the appointment and that you choose foods so that they are both spoon-fed or both finger-fed. For example, if you spoon-fed your child during the first appointment, we ask that you also spoon-feed for the second appointment.

name: Babies' Relationships with Their Caregivers · Children Helping Science
description: In this study, we are interested in whether babies form expectations about what their caregivers will do next. Prior research suggests that babies are sensitive to social interactions, but we still know relatively little about how they understand the social goals of their parents and caregivers. In this study, we will have you look toward one puppet over another and ask whether babies would be more surprised when you’ll look toward a new puppet, depending on how the puppets had responded to you. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Participate Now!” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. Before the study, we will ask you to make a short 15-second video using your webcam. For the video, you need to say ’Hey Baby’ as though you’re talking to your baby. If you agree to participate, we will send you instructions on making the video, which should take under 5 minutes to create. In this Zoom-based study, your baby will watch videos of you. We’re interested in how babies understand about the social interactions of their caregivers.

Metadata reproduced for non-commercial cataloging only — consult the lab and the linked landing page for licence and consent terms.

name: Baby Friends & Feelings · Children Helping Science
description: Research shows babies expect friends, but not rivals, to be happy for each other when one succeeds. We don't know whether babies expect the reverse: whether empathy (or being happy for someone's success) signals friendship, while counter-empathy (or being sad for someone's success) signals rivalry. In this study, we ask just that: do babies use empathy as a cue for a positive social relationship? This study will help us better understand how babies reason about friendship, empathy, and their relationship to each other. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. This study asks if babies expect characters who are empathetic to each other to be friends or rivals. Your baby will watch as one of the characters responds positively to one character's success, but sadly to another character. Then your baby will see the characters interact positively with each other. We will use gaze tracking to ask: Do babies expect someone who empathizes to also be friends with the individual they empathized with?
credits: Source: Children Helping Science — public study registry hosted at https://childrenhelpingscience.com. Run by Little Learners Lab (Lehigh University). Listed at https://childrenhelpingscience.com/studies/aa4ff6ed-6710-4e29-9e19-8d04d9863ef8/. Participation requires a Children Helping Science family account; running the study then happens on the lab's own platform (Lookit, Zoom, Qualtrics, etc.). Metadata reproduced for non-commercial cataloging only — consult the lab and the linked landing page for licence and consent terms.

name: Baby Problem Solvers · Children Helping Science
description: Babies expect that people will be happy when they accomplish a goal or succeed at a task, but they struggle to understand what should happen when goals are incomplete or failed. This may be a result of not having a clear reason for why an action has failed. In this study, we show babies a large obstacle that prevents a character from completing their goal and ask whether having salient information leads to clearer emotion predictions when actions are failed. This study will help us better understand how babies reason about different outcomes and how these outcomes relate to their understanding of emotions. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. This study asks if babies expect characters to respond differently when they succeed versus when they fail. Your baby will see a colorful shape attempt to jump over a tall wall. On the other side of the wall will be a mat that the character wants to get to or an even larger wall that is blocking the mat. The character will either succeed in getting to the mat or fail due to the additional obstacle. Then your baby will see the character respond happily or sadly to their outcome. We will use gaze tracking to ask: Do babies expect others to be sad when they fail and happy when they succeed?
credits: Source: Children Helping Science — public study registry hosted at https://childrenhelpingscience.com. Run by Little Learners Lab (Lehigh University). Listed at https://childrenhelpingscience.com/studies/e3702b25-7151-4929-855c-d2c7dbc2f92b/. Participation requires a Children Helping Science family account; running the study then happens on the lab's own platform (Lookit, Zoom, Qualtrics, etc.). name: Blue dots, yellow dots! · Children Helping Science
description: Persisting in the face of challenge is central to learning. However, we cannot and should not persist at everything! How do children decide what is worth their effort? Here, we look at whether children decide whether to keep going based on how they think about their past failure - was it due to something in their control or not? Ultimately, we hope to uncover the factors that lead to increased persistence in the face of failure when it might matter most. Your child will complete four trials of a dot-counting task to try out for a "club". At first, your child will learn that they did not get into the club, but they will get into the club later! Your child will answer questions about their own ability and choose whether they want to practice or try again. At the end of the study, we will explain that the reason that they did not get into the club at first was due to an error in the system and then celebrate their success so that all children leave feeling proud of their performance.

Participation requires a Children Helping Science family account; running the study then happens on the lab's own platform (Lookit, Zoom, Qualtrics, etc.). Metadata reproduced for non-commercial cataloging only — consult the lab and the linked landing page for licence and consent terms.

name: Bonding: A survey for parents · Children Helping Science
description: Bonding refers to the connection between children and their caregivers. It is an important foundation for child and family wellbeing. The goal of this study is to create a better way of measuring relationships. Existing surveys about bonding are commonly used to capture how parents feel about their baby. This study will capture bonding in parents and children of an older age group and a wider range (3 to 10 years). A comprehensive survey about bonding will help us understand different ways that parents and children form bonds and ultimately help us test how healthy bonding supports children's development. You will be asked to rate statements about your family, including questions about your behaviors, your child's behaviors, and your feelings about your child. For example, you will be asked to rate how much you agree with statements such as, "If my child is upset, they will seek comfort from me" and "I speak to my child in a warm and friendly voice." This survey is for grown-ups, so your child does not have to be present while you complete it.

Metadata reproduced for non-commercial cataloging only — consult the lab and the linked landing page for licence and consent terms.

name: Bumping Shapes · Children Helping Science
description: We’re interested in how babies understand cause-and-effect events. When one object bumps into another, sometimes it makes the other object move, and sometimes it doesn’t. By showing babies a series of these events, we hope to learn whether they can pick up on the patterns or rules that determine which shapes can make things happen. By measuring how long your baby looks at these events when the rule was violated or confirmed, we can better understand how babies make sense of these simple rules. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. During the study, your baby will watch cheerful animated videos featuring colorful shapes moving across the screen. Some shapes will bump into gray squares and make them move, while others won’t. We’ll record what your baby looks at and for how long using your webcam.

name: Can babies generate explanations? · Children Helping Science
description: Explanations are crucial for adults' reasoning abilities, but there has been little work done on explanation capabilities in children, and almost none in babies. Previous studies have suggested that babies can search for and recognize explanations, but can they generate them? Here we test that question! This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. Your baby will watch videos depicting one of two events: a ball bouncing off of a wall, or appearing to pass through a wall, where the full shape of the wall (with either a hole on the bottom or on the top) is initially hidden from view. We will then test whether babies can generate an explanation for this event by seeing if they look longer at the wall that can explain the event that they saw.

name: Can babies guess the next dance move? · Children Helping Science
description: Previous research has shown that babies can use a small amount of evidence about objects to make predictions about future events. We are interested if this type of reasoning can be applied to make future predictions about social groups. We will measure this by seeing if babies can predict the last group's actions when it's their turn to dance after seeing three other groups dance previously! This will help us better understand the development of children’s reasoning about social groups and their behaviors. Your baby will watch short videos of four groups of animated characters dancing. Each group will first have its members dance with each other and then dance towards a box. We're curious to see if babies can predict which box the fourth group will dance towards after seeing how the previous three groups danced.
credits: Source: Children Helping Science — public study registry hosted at https://childrenhelpingscience.com. Run by Berkeley Early Learning Lab (UC Berkeley). Listed at https://childrenhelpingscience.com/studies/2f936d79-ab51-4d2a-8d2a-2844160f5023/. Participation requires a Children Helping Science family account; running the study then happens on the lab's own platform (Lookit, Zoom, Qualtrics, etc.). Metadata reproduced for non-commercial cataloging only — consult the lab and the linked landing page for licence and consent terms.

name: Can you choose the cupcake she wants? · Children Helping Science
description: Children are undergoing very rapid language acquisition in the late preschool years, figuring out all kinds of complex forms including negatives, multiple adjectives, and stress patterns. Little is known about children's understanding of how negatives, multiple adjectives, and stress patterns jointly determine what someone is referring to. The study will allow us to answer some preliminary questions about the age at which children understand them like adults, and if not, what they do instead. In this study, your child will hear a story about a character choosing cupcakes and teddybears, and your child will have to figure out which one the character means from a set. In each case, the narrator will tell the participant what the character wants, but using indirect description, to see at what age children can figure out what is meant. For example, if I tell you "I don't want the GREEN hat", you might infer that I want a different color hat, rather than no hat at all. When do children do this?
credits: Source: Children Helping Science — public study registry hosted at https://childrenhelpingscience.com. Run by Smith College Lab (Smith College). Listed at https://childrenhelpingscience.com/studies/7ff3ca55-5f9c-41d4-9e43-a97e7d34c22e/. name: Can you find the rose? · Children Helping Science
description: We want to understand how the amount of knowledge children have about categories changes over time. As children get older, they may have more information about a certain category. By showing children objects that vary in their similarity to each other, we can see if children at a certain age have enough knowledge about a category to recognize the labeled image even when it is paired with a similar object. If we understand how this process changes across development, we can provide better assessments and interventions for children who struggle with early category learning, including late talkers and children with ASD. Your child will look at several pairs of images placed side-by-side, varying in their similarity to each other, and will be prompted by an audio recording to look at one of the images! Then, you will be prompted to fill out a survey in which you answer questions about whether your child understands and says different words. Your child's face will be recorded during the study and we will use their eye movements to understand which images they're looking at.

name: Can you roar like a tiger? · Children Helping Science
description: Our lab studies whether babies are intrinsically curious about what their developing minds and bodies can do. To test this question, we’ve designed a series of virtual games that babies can operate just by interacting with the camera. The purpose of this study is to playtest these games to make sure that they are fun and developmentally appropriate for 16- to 18- month olds. This study will take place on a live video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Participate Now!” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. During this study, your baby will play interactive games using your camera, such as pressing a digital button on the screen using the spacebar. At the end, you will also be asked to complete a short survey about your baby’s motor development.

name: Can You See It? · Children Helping Science
description: This study will tell us if hearing voice cues (such as “Look, a dog!”) help babies recognize partly covered pictures. We will compare where babies look when they hear speech to where they look when they hear speech played backwards. By measuring the time babies spend looking at the covered or the scrambled picture we can tell if they have “spotted” the picture. Ultimately, this study will help us learn how babies who are just starting to learn language might use the words they hear to better understand different objects in their visual world! During this study your baby will be shown image pairs. One side will have a partly covered version of a picture and the other side will have a scrambled up version of the picture. The pictures will appear for 5 seconds each. Before each picture, there will be one of two audio cues: one will be backwards audio, the other will be a voice cue that matches the picture. We will time your child's eye movements while they listen to the sound and look at the pictures!

name: Children’s experiences with AD(H)D medication · Children Helping Science
description: The goal of this study is for children to express how they feel and think about their medication. There has been very little research on the subjective experiences of children, and we believe this is an important part of understanding medication use in children. This study is for children who take AD(H)D medication such as Ritalin or Concerta. Your child will fill out questions about their experiences before and after starting their medication. Example questions include “Did your doctor take your blood pressure before prescribing you the medicine?” and “How often have you thought about nice things since starting the medication?”.

name: Children's Reasoning about Diversity · Children Helping Science
description: Studying how children reason about diverse groups of people furthers our understanding of their reactions to diversity and factors that contribute to acceptance and support for diversity. This can have implications for education, policy-making, and early interventions to promote positive attitudes towards diversity. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. During the study, your child will learn about various fictional characters. First, they will be shown groups of diverse fictional characters and will be asked how much they like each group. Then, they will learn about fictional characters who possess cultural or occupational knowledge. Your child will be asked questions about what they heard and saw.

name: Children's reasoning about exceptional events · Children Helping Science
description: The purpose of this study is to help us better understand how children think about events in the past and how they could have turned out differently. This type of thinking is called counterfactual thinking. It involves imagining alternatives to past events by making changes to the event. When we think counterfactually, some events are easier to “undo” to change the outcome of past events than others. Previous research suggests that one of these events we tend to be biased to undo is exceptional events. For example, if you were to take the train to work when you usually drive, and then the train gets delayed so you don’t make it to work on time, most adults will tend to think “if only I had driven to work as usual, then I would not have been late”. In this study, we seek to determine whether children tend to show the same reasoning bias towards exceptional events that adults show. This study will take place over a video call with a researcher. Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. In this study, your child will be watching a series of short videos about a character who deviates from their normal routine. Your child will be answering short questions related to each of the stories. Before the study session, you will receive an online consent form and brief demographic survey, which you will need to fill out before participation.

name: Children's Word Learning Strategies · Children Helping Science
description: The study aims to understand what strategies help 3-5-year-old children learn a second language most effectively and the influences on word learning. In this study, we want to know how effective pictures, sentence structures, and translations are for learning words in a second language. We also want to know if children's language backgrounds and characteristics can affect these three word learning strategies. This research might help families and teachers interested in supporting children's second-language learning. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Participate Now!” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. You will then receive a Google Meet invitation, an online consent form, and a brief demographic survey by email. In a Google Meet session (≤ 30 minutes) children will be taught basic words in Spanish by use of a picture, a sentence, or a translation into English and then be asked to identify the meaning of those words.

name: Come Drum With Me! · Children Helping Science
description: We’re studying how preschoolers learn to move in time with someone else during a drumming activity. Understanding what helps children synchronize (like familiar partners or simpler rhythms) is important because shared timing and coordination are thought to play a role in how kids connect, communicate, and cooperate with others. For this study, you will need something your child can use as a drum — this could be a toy drum, a real drum, or an everyday object like an upside-down bowl or pot. At the beginning of the study, you will complete a short questionnaire with questions such as whether your child has attended early music, dance, or drama classes, and whether they regularly hear music at home. After that, your child will watch a video and take part in a simple drumming activity with animated characters on the screen. They will see different characters who drum at different speeds and with different rhythms.

name: Commitment Study for Adult Dogs · Children Helping Science
description: We are investigating whether dogs understand commitments like humans do. The aim of this study is to determine whether dogs show behaviours indicative of understanding commitments by investigating how dogs react when their owner makes a commitment to do a shared activity but does not follow through with the commitment. You would record yourself and your dog getting ready to go out for a walk on one occasion. Then, once a week for 3 weeks, you would record yourself complete one of the following 5-minute procedures. For procedure 1, you would ask your dog "do you want to go for a walk?", pick up their dog-walking materials, but then set them back down and ignore your dog for 90 seconds. For procedure 2, you would not speak or look at your dog while picking up their dog-walking materials, and then, you would set them back down and ignore your dog for 90 seconds. For procedure 3, you would not speak or look at your dog while picking up an object, such as a book, and then, you would set it back down and ignore your dog for 90 seconds. After completing the consent form, you will be redirected to our lab's website where you will find the instructions on how to complete the study on your own without a researcher present.

name: Communication during infant and parent play · Children Helping Science
description: Babies learn to communicate through everyday back-and-forth moments with caregivers — like when a baby makes a sound and someone responds. This study explores how these responsive interactions help babies learn new vocal skills. We are especially interested in understanding whether babies learn differently depending on how adults respond to them. By studying these early learning processes, we hope to better understand how communication develops in the first year of life. This research may help scientists learn more about early social development and, in the long term, inform ways to better support children who may be at risk for communication challenges. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. In this scheduled live Zoom session, you and your baby will play together while a researcher observes and provides simple instructions through an earpiece. During some parts of the session, you will respond to your baby’s vocalizations in specific ways (such as smiling, speaking, or gently interacting). During other parts, you will continue playing as you normally would. The session lasts about 40 minutes and includes short play periods. After signing up, we will send you a Zoom link and simple setup instructions before the session. You will need a laptop with Zoom and bluetooth earphones so you can hear the researcher’s instructions. No preparation is required, and you can stop at any time.

name: Conversations on Household Labor Between Children and Their Parents · Children Helping Science
description: Previous research suggests that children’s concepts of fairness are largely influenced by their household experience. We are interested in discovering how children discuss fairness in the context of household labor with their parents and how these conversations may shape their own ideas of fairness in the household (i.e. how conversations about household labor may influence how children choose to divide chores when given the opportunity). This study will take place on a Zoom video call with a researcher! Selecting “Schedule a time to participate” will take you to a booking website, where you can select a time and date that works best for you. During the study, you and your child will be shown two videos about two different families doing different activities in the house. You will be asked to discuss together what each member of the family is doing and why. You and your child will then discuss a few questions about how chores are divided in your own family. Finally, your child will be presented with a series of different chores and will be asked to distribute them between two hypothetical parents based on how they usually see the chores distributed and how they think the chores should be distributed between the parents.

name: Cue the Music! (Chinese) · Children Helping Science
description: During the first year of life, infants are rapidly learning their primary language(s). Previous research has shown that children use different aspects of culture as cues in this process of learning a language, and for the bilingual speaker, in keeping the two languages apart. We are looking to see whether the cultural cue of music will have an effect on the infant’s ability to differentiate between language-specific speech sounds. For bilingual-learning babies hearing English and Chinese. In this study, your baby will first be enjoying short clips of women singing a tune to them (and hopefully you'll enjoy it as well!) in English, Cantonese, or Mandarin. Then, your child will listen to a series of language sounds while looking at rainbow dots. Note: Please use the Firefox browser, a laptop, and view the preview before proceeding.

name: Dónde está la ball? · Children Helping Science
description: Many bilingual people switch between two languages and might speak one of them with an accent. For example, someone who speaks Spanish as their first language might speak English with a Spanish accent. We're curious if having an accent makes it harder to notice when someone switches languages. This study tests specifically if Spanish-English bilingual children have a harder time noticing when someone switches languages if the speaker has an accent. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Participate Now!” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. Your child will watch and listen to a 3-minute video with sentences with language switches and be asked by an accented speaker to look at different objects on the screen. You may also be asked to participate in a short 25-minute structured conversation, via phone call or Zoom, about your child’s language exposure.

name: Deciding which proportion is bigger! · Children Helping Science
description: Proportions come in so many different shapes and sizes, yet children and adults are often easily able to compare them! A single trip through the grocery store will have you estimating which trail mix packages is made up of more raisins or how much flour you need to double your cake recipe. Previous research has shown that the way proportional information is presented visually has a big effect on the way you interpret them. In this study, we are interested in how people's ability to make decisions with proportions changes as children get older and when the proportions look different. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. Once the call begins our researcher will show your child images of proportion amounts, in the form of orange and blue shapes (like rectangles) that might differ in how they look, and to make decisions about them. For example, we will ask them to compare the proportion of different colors, predict the outcomes of different events (like throwing ball at different pictures) based on probability, or match proportions across images.

name: Do I prefer someone inside my group or outside my group? · Children Helping Science
description: In this study we are interested in children’s preferences on who should be the leader of a classroom when half of the classroom chooses the participant’s in-group leader and the other half chooses an out-group leader. Previous research tells us that during this developmental age, children align strongly with their in-group (even if briefly assigned) and prefer those that are friendly over unfriendly towards others. Therefore, we are especially interested in how children’s preferences for leadership differs when they see evidence of their in-group members acting unfriendly towards other students. Does the behavior from the in-group members extend to who they endorse? (the in-group leader). This study will give us more insight on children’s decision making when choosing leadership especially between unfriendly in-group members and neutral out-groups. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you During the Zoom meeting you will meet one of our researchers, and they will tell you a story about students in a classroom that need to select one leader. The story will align your child with one of two groups and ask them who should be the leader of the classroom, their in-group leader or the out-group leader? We will then show the participating child negative events (e.g. student not sharing cookies) involving either their in-group candidate or another student. Finally, participants have to once again make the choice of who should be the leader of the classroom.

name: Do, Re, Remember! · Children Helping Science
description: The goal of this study is to learn how babies remember different sounds. We are looking to see if infants can tell the difference between instruments that sound alike and instruments that sound different, and how long they can remember those sounds. This project will help us learn more about the development of auditory working memory in babies and how these skills begin to develop early in life. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. During the study, your child will look at a screen and listen to instruments playing. We will record your child’s eye movements to see for how long they are listening to the instruments. Important note: Because we are looking at eye movements, we ask that you join the Zoom meeting from a laptop or desktop computer (not mobile or tablet). We might also ask you to turn lights on or off during the session so that we can better see your baby's eyes.

name: Do you like the person you can predict? · Children Helping Science
description: Babies rapidly navigate the social world. Previous research suggests that infants are drawn to novel or dynamic events but also show preferences for reliable people, such as their caregivers. Building on this work, the study asks whether infants prefer individuals who behave predictably (a cue to reliability) or whether perceptual novelty overrides predictability. This study will take place over a live video call with a researcher. Clicking the “Schedule a time to participate” button will take you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. During the session, your baby will watch videos of one person who consistently likes or dislikes an object, and a second person who alternates between the two. Afterward, your baby will see both people on the same screen. Babies often show preferences by looking longer, so we will observe how long your baby looks at each person to determine whether they prefer someone with a more consistent and predictable reaction.

name: Fashion Detectives: How Babies Use Clothing as a Social Signal · Children Helping Science
description: This study asks whether babies use style of dress to form expectations about people, such as whether they share other meaningful qualities. By answering this question, we hope to learn more about how early in life humans begin to look for signals of group membership and use those signals to understand the social world around them. This study will take place during a live video call with a researcher. You will need to share your screen during the video call. Clicking the “Schedule a time to participate” button will take you to an online calendar where you can choose a date and time that works for your family. During the Zoom session, the researcher will send you a link in the Zoom chat that opens a short presentation with the videos your baby will watch. The presentation needs to play on your screen so your baby can see it clearly. The researcher will control the presentation, but we will ask you to share your screen so we can make sure the videos are playing correctly and that everyone is viewing the same thing at the same time. For this reason, it is important that your computer settings allow screen sharing. Your baby will watch short videos of women wearing traditional clothing who speak Portuguese and Japanese, so it is important that your baby does not regularly hear Japanese or Portuguese at home. We are interested in seeing which person your baby looks at longer. This helps us learn more about how babies pay attention to how people look and how they speak.

name: Feminist Parenting of Young Children · Children Helping Science
description: This study helps us learn about why and how feminist parents discuss topics around gender with their young children. This is an important topic to research so we can design interventions to support gender-diverse children. Existing research has explored conversations that parents have with adolescents on gender, but research with feminist parents and parents of younger children is missing. Your participation in this study will include a) completing an online survey about gender socialization and parenting (e.g., Can you give concrete examples of how you discuss gender with your child?) and b) select participants will be mailed a book about gender, and asked to audio-record themselves reading the book and discussing it with their child. Recordings will be conducted on participants’ phones and uploaded to a secure, private link provided by the researcher. Filling out the online survey does not guarantee selection for the book portion of the study.

name: Follow Me, Friends! · Children Helping Science
description: From a young age, we are able to recognize that people who are part of the same social group tend to imitate one another's behaviors. However, acting in a similar way is not always socially motivated. For example, sometimes we'll act in the same way as another person in order to reach a goal. This study investigates whether babies think that a character, represented by a simple, 2D animated shape, will prefer to approach another character whose movement it imitates over a third character whose movement it does not imitate. It further investigates whether infants' expectations change whether or not the imitating character's action allows it to reach a goal object. This project is part of a larger study which seeks to build a benchmark for understanding babies' social intuitions. This study will take place on a video call live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. In this study, your baby will watch a ~5-minute animated video of simple geometric shapes interacting in a maze-like environment. In particular, one of these shapes will imitate the movement of only one of the other two shapes. We will measure how long your baby looks at the different behaviors of these shapes.

name: For Children in Three US States (CA, NY, or TX): Fairy Tales! · Children Helping Science
description: We are checking to make sure that the Fairy Tale Test works to assess children’s personality, imagination and creativity. We also want to know how children differ from each other, by age, by gender, and where they live. Other researchers are collecting data in different languages all over the world. We are collecting data in Texas, New York, and California. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. On the call, your child will look at illustrations from fairy tales and tell us about their characters: Snow White, Little Red Riding Hood, and Jack & the Beanstalk. Then we will ask the child to play quick video games to show us their vocabulary (name the pictures) and inhibition (tap the fish not the shark) which are related to creativity and imagination. Parents will also answer a brief survey about their child’s imagination, personality and provide demographic information at the end.

name: Guess feelings over time! · Children Helping Science
description: How do children feel when they don't know what’s going to happen next? In this study, we are interested in learning how children think others' feelings might change over prolonged periods of certainty and uncertainty. We will first tell your child some stories about children receiving certain (positive or negative) or uncertain news and then they’ll be asked to rate how the child in each story might feel at different points in time. This study will help us understand how children predict changes in others' feelings based on the certainty or uncertainty of the situation. Your child will hear stories about other children waiting to hear some important news. The person will then receive either positive, negative, or uncertain news. Your child will be asked to rate how the child in each story might feel at different time points using the emotion scale provided and provide a brief explanation for their ratings.

name: Happiness: A survey for parents · Children Helping Science
description: Babies express themselves in many ways like smiles and laughter. These positive expressions are important foundations for their future development and well-being. The goal of this study is to create a survey that captures babies’ emotions. Existing surveys about baby emotions often require in-person visits or focus on older children. This study will try to capture babies’ (2 months to 2 years) emotions using a survey completed by a parent or caregiver. Measuring babies’ emotions, such as happiness, will help us understand their emotions and learn new ways to help them grow into happier and healthier adults. You will be asked to rate statements about your family, including questions about your baby's behaviors and feelings. For example, you will be asked to rate how often your baby expresses certain behaviors and emotions, such as being "happy," "excited," or "laughing." This survey is for grown-ups, so your baby does not have to be present while you complete it.

name: Help Momo Learn Numbers! · Children Helping Science
description: Counting is one of the hallmark abilities young children achieve in early childhood. How do their understanding of number words grow over time? In this study we have developed a fun series of games that will help document your child's number skills and experiences over time. Findings from this study will give us new insights to how number knowledge emerges and grows in each child. In this study, your child will go on an adventure with an alien friend! Your child will complete a series of games by using keyboard to demonstrate their understanding of number words. You and your child will also briefly talk about a picture together, like during reading time. After that you, the parent, will be completing a questionnaire. We will invite you to come back and participate again in the future, so that we can also study how your child's number skills change over time. This study works best with an external keyboard. If you are using a laptop, please have a towel handy to cover the touchpad during the key-pressing games.

name: Hey, I know what that means! · Children Helping Science
description: In this study, we’re interested in what very young children know about the words we use to describe properties of things in the world. Some of these words are harder to learn and take longer to acquire in child development, because they place restrictions on the kind of objects and individuals they can describe—and this is the kind of thing we don’t teach children! For example, only animals and people can be sleepy or alert. Chairs and plants can't be! We want to know if two-year-olds are aware of these restrictions, because this will help us to know what exactly they learn when they first learn these words, and what might have to be acquired later on. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. During the study, your child will interact with a live researcher and a friendly alien named Weepo. Weepo would like to know if your child knows the meaning of some words he has learned. Your child will see some images, then Weepo will describe one, and ask your child to find it.

name: Hi / Salut / Namaste! My parent’s friends are my friends · Children Helping Science
description: This is a follow-up to a study that found that children prefer characters that speak the same language as them. This study aims to explore whether children overcome this language bias when they see adults interacting with characters that speak a different language to them. This is important for understanding children’s friendship preferences based on language, and exploring ways to build linguistic diversity. Before your appointment, you will be asked to film and send us a short, 15-second video of yourself that will be edited and shown to your child during the study. To schedule the appointment click on “Schedule a time to participate” to see an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. During the scheduled time, your child will watch pairs of puppets speaking different languages and will be asked which one they would prefer to have as their friend. They will also be shown edited videos of you, their caregiver, and another adult interacting with these puppets and will be asked a few questions about the puppets.

name: How are these people feeling? · Children Helping Science
description: We are interested in understanding how children's own emotional states influence how they perceive emotional information in other people. Everyone needs to be able to detect and recognize emotions in others to build and maintain social relationships. In both adults and children, feeling happy, calm, stressed, or lonely can influence how they recognize emotions in others. However, it is not clear exactly how this occurs. In this study, we hope to clarify some of these findings by looking more generally at how feelings like loneliness influence positive and negative ratings of faces. A better understanding of this could aid in developing supports for children at risk of high levels of loneliness. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! This study involves five scheduled sessions over the year, each session 2 months apart. Each session should take around 45 minutes to complete. To schedule the first session, click on the “Schedule a time to participate” button to see an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. In this study, your child will first answer some questions about how they have been feeling and thinking recently. They will then play a game with the researcher in which they see pictures or videos of people and will be asked how they are feeling. We will also ask you, the caregiver, some questions about your family and life experiences. These will include questions about significant events experienced by the family, family routines and day-to-day functioning, and current mood and stress levels.

name: How Babies Learn from What They See AND Hear at the Same Time · Children Helping Science
description: Researchers have found that babies learn better by combining information from different senses, such as seeing and hearing at the same time. This ability is called multisensory processing, and it may play an important role in how children develop language and social skills. However, we still know little about how these skills develop during the first years of life and how early differences may relate to later development. In this study, we explore how babies pay attention to things they see and hear at the same time, such as moving objects or people speaking. By observing where babies look while watching short videos, we aim to understand more about how these early attention skills relate to later language and social development. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. For this study, you will need a tape measure and two sticky notes.In this study, children and parents will join a 45-minute Zoom call with the research team. We will ask you to measure the distance from the screen to ensure that all children in the study are positioned at the same distance from the computer. We will also ask you to sit outside of your child’s view or cover your eyes with sticky notes. We ask this because you will be playing an “at-home experimenter” role, and it is important that caregivers remain blind to the study conditions.Your baby will watch short videos of moving objects or people speaking while we observe where they look on the screen. Some videos will show two or six women telling a story. Others will include objects. In the object videos, small colorful items (such as tokens, cubes, or dice) will drop into two jars, or six different objects will appear. After each short video, babies will see a smiling face or a moving ball.In this study, we explore how babies pay attention to things they see and hear at the same time. While babies see multiple objects or people on the screen, they hear a single sound that matches only one of them. By observing where babies look, we examine whether they pay more attention to the matching object or person, and whether this differs between social (people) and non-social (objects) situations.

name: How babies think about surprising objects · Children Helping Science
description: Previous studies have shown that babies understand physical principles an object should follow. For example, they look longer at a ball if it appears to pass through a solid wall, showing that babies recognize that objects should be solid and not go through each other. In this study we want to explore how infants think about objects that behave in these kinds of surprising ways. After seeing an object behave unexpectedly, do they still expect this object to obey physical rules? To find out, we will compare babies’ interest in a toy that does two surprising things in comparison to just one. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher. Clicking on the "Schedule a time to participate" button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. We will show your baby a series of videos short videos showing some toys performing magical or normal actions. We are interested in how your baby reacts to the toys after observing the events!

name: How children think about care and social relationships · Children Helping Science
description: This study investigates how young children understand caregiving relationships. Do children see these relationships as different from other kinds of relationships? What do children expect helping to look like in caregiving relationships? Even very young children expect family and friends to help more than strangers, but prior work has not examined the kinds of relationships that children think involve altruistic helping (that is, if children understand that caregivers often incur costs when helping the cared-for). This study will help us better understand how children think about caregiving relationships. This study takes place on a Zoom video call with a researcher! Clicking on the “schedule a time to participate” button will take you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works best for you and your child. Your child will listen to stories about characters who help each other. Based on patterns of helping, we will ask your child to make guesses about the characters’ relationship. For instance, one character (who is hungry themselves) may help another by giving them a snack, and we will ask your child whether they think the character is the parent.

name: How Do Children Learn the Number Five? · Children Helping Science
description: As adults, we can track any number of objects exactly. We can easily tell a set of 9 balls is different from a set of 10 balls by counting the two sets, breaking them into small sets, or putting them into one-to-one correspondence. However, this ability does not come for free. Research has shown that infants and young children can only track up to three or four objects exactly. When in development do children come to track large exact sets and what supports their tracking ability? We aim to address these questions in a case study on the number five. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. In this study, your child will play two games about numbers. In the first game, they will see several stars go into an empty box and then part or all of them come out. They will be asked whether all the stars have come out. In the second game, they will be asked to put certain numbers of objects onto a plate. To do this, we will need your help to prepare ten identical smallish objects (such as beads or quarters) and a plate.

name: How Do Kids With Autism See Faces? · Children Helping Science
description: Faces play an important role in social communication. To interact successfully with others, children need to recognize and tell people’s faces apart. In this study, we are interested in how children with autism spectrum disorder perceive faces. We hope to learn more about how face perception develops in kids with autism and how it supports children’s social understanding and communication. If your child has a professional diagnosis of autism, we invite you to do our study! In this study, your child will see two faces on the screen. The top parts of the faces will either be the same or different. Your child will be asked to decide whether the top halves of the faces are the same or different. At the end, we will ask you to fill out some questionnaires about your child's diagnosis. This study will help us better understand the development of face perception in kids with autism.

name: How many languages do bilingual babies expect others to speak? · Children Helping Science
description: Children in North America are learning language in a socially (e.g., ethnicity) and linguistically (e.g., accents) diverse environment. Therefore, we hope to better understand how these factors influence the assumptions toddlers make about new speakers. Specifically, in this study we will see if toddlers are surprised when the same person speaks more than one language, and whether this depends on how familiar they are with people of that person’s race. This study will provide us insight into how social characteristics (i.e., race) interact with toddlers assumptions about language and the linguistically diverse environment they see around them. This study will take place on a website called Labvanced, which (similar to Lookit) will use your webcam and microphone while the study takes place on screen. First, you will complete a demographics questionnaire. Then, your toddler will see people of different races speaking different languages. We will see how long your toddler looks at each person.
