name: I see me, I see you: Recognizing emotions in others · Children Helping Science
description: Our study explores when and how preschoolers with autism recognize different kinds of emotions from other peoples' facial expressions! This study includes some real-world contexts, like distracting background sounds, to reflect how children recognize emotions in the real world! Our study will also explore how children's emotion learning may change after viewing one’s own emotion expressions. For example, does a child seeing themself laugh help them recognize happy faces? Some studies have shown that older children and adults are better at identifying emotion expressions after watching themselves express similar emotions. This part of our study will help identify key tools for young children with emotion processing challenges. Half of this study takes place on a scheduled video call with a researcher! The other half of this study consists of surveys that will take about 20 to 45 minutes. Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online form where you can confirm your eligibility and schedule a date and time that works for you! We will send you a link to a survey that has questions about you, your child, and their sensory experiences, developmental milestones, and other behaviors. During the 45-minute study session, your child will look at pictures of other children and answer some questions about these children's feelings. Your child will also watch video clips, from television shows and movies, like Moana, and later watch their reactions to these videos (e.g., child laughing or frowning).

name: Infants' expectations of people's and object's movements · Children Helping Science
description: We are interested in infants' expectations regarding the movement behaviors of people and objects. In this study, one object or person will move from the left to behind an occluder in the center of the screen and a new object or person will start behind the occluder and then move to the right of the screen. We will later remove the occluder and show infants that the people and objects are making contact behind the occluder or not making contact. We are interested in how infants looking times may differ depending on expectations of what is happening behind the occluder. The primary question we aim to answer is whether infants have different expectations of the cause of movement in objects versus people. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. After finding a time that works for you, you will be directed to survey where you can complete our consent form and answer some research relevant questions. During the Zoom session, we’ll show your child some animated videos of shapes or people moving across a screen before stopping behind an occluder in the center of the screen. Then after a brief delay a new animated object or person will appear from behind the occluder and move the rest of the way across the screen. While we show these videos we will be recording your child's looking times. We are curious about what infants expect might be going on behind the central screen.

name: Infants' Understanding of Shared Emotional Experiences · Children Helping Science
description: Previous research suggests that infants prefer individuals who provide social support. We want to further study infants’ understanding of social support. We are curious about whether babies think people are closer friends when they spend time with someone who is feeling sad, compared to when they spend time with someone who is feeling happy. This study will present infants with a character who is responsive to a protagonist’s distress, and another character who is instead responsive to the same protagonist’s happiness. We will examine whether infants expect the protagonist to prefer the character who was responsive to their distress. This research will shed light on how infants think about the sharing of emotions within close relationships. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Participate Now!” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. In this Zoom-based study, your child will watch animations featuring characters (3D shapes with eyes) who respond to each other, as the characters are either sad or happy.

name: Is that possible? · Children Helping Science
description: In this study, we are interested in understanding how children’s cognitive control and cognitive reflection (the ability to reflect on their intuitive answers) relate to how they think about events (impossible, impermissible) that we don’t typically see around us (events that are impossible; immoral events). We additionally want to understand how participants’ linguality (whether they are monolingual or bilingual) impacts their thinking about these events. The study will take place via Zoom with a researcher! Selecting “Schedule a time to participate” takes you to a booking website, where you can choose a date and time that works best for you. A live researcher will tell your child about some actions that other children want to do that break physical or social rules (e.g., walk through walls, hit someone else) and ask them whether they think it is okay or not okay. They will also take part in a game where they raise their different hands depending on whether hearts or flowers appear on the screen, and will be asked a set of short brain teasers (e.g., what do cows drink?).

name: Is this blicket ZORPY? · Children Helping Science
description: We are interested in how sentence structure helps kids figure out the meanings of new words. For example, if you hear something like "The blick was crying," you are likely to think that blick is something animate; if you hear "She was blicking all night," you are likely to think that blick is some kind of action or event. In this particular study, we are looking at how sentence structure might help kids figure out the meaning of adjectives-- words like "smart" or "tall." In this asynchronous study, you and your child will watch short video clips of conversations between people which use some made-up words. Your child will guess what some of the made-up words mean. You will help them navigate through the study and type their responses. There will be a total of 6 words that your child has to guess.

name: Is This One? · Children Helping Science
description: From infancy, we pay attention to how people move around the world and what their goals are. We are interested in whether children use such prelinguistic intuitions to guess the meanings of new words that apply to people and their actions. We use very simple scenarios to try to isolate different properties of others’ actions that might intuitively draw children’s attention during word learning. The results from this study will help us understand how children learn new words that describe the world around them. NOTE: THIS EXPERIMENT MIGHT TAKE 30-60 SECONDS TO LOAD. During this time you will see a blank screen. After the experiment beings, your child will watch short videos of simple shapes moving in a simple environment, and they will hear new words used to describe what they see. Then, your child will be asked whether these new words apply to certain actions in new videos. Your child will provide a 'yes' or 'no' response, and there are no right or wrong answers.

name: Join Adventures with Amy, Ben, and other friends! · Children Helping Science
description: The purpose of our study is to examine how children reason about causal roles, particularly in situations that involve mental states, like emotions. We are specifically interested in how intuitions manifest in cases with multiple causal agents, as well as those in which an agent's lack of action leads to the outcome in question. In this study, we are looking at cases involving causal chains; wherein one agent's action affects another agent and that plays a causal role in the outcome. This will help us learn more about how children interpret causes and responsibility in situations where multiple actors are involved. In our study, your child will watch a brief animated video and answer a few questions. The video will introduce characters and describe a situation, and then your child will be asked about which characters are causally responsible for the outcomes.

name: Judgement of Disagreement · Children Helping Science
description: Children have different approaches to evaluating and resolving disagreements about uncertain scientific scenarios. Some trust peer informants while others do not draw conclusions based on peer informants. In this study, we are interested in seeing whether these approaches follow a developmental timetable and what social factors influence the developmental pattern. This research can help educators and parents better teach and scaffold children's constructive approaches to disagreements as they navigate their world. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. Your child will be presented with uncertain scientific scenarios that have different outcomes; for example, they may see two objects of uncertain weight and be asked questions about other people's predictions.

name: Learning about hidden creatures · Children Helping Science
description: We want to understand how babies' ability to imagine and name an object they have never seen based on a few familiar images (measured at 14-16 months), helps them learn detailed new information from language at 22-24 months. This will help us better understand how babies begin to make a link between language and knowledge and learn about things they have never experienced from language. Down the road, this understanding will also help other scientists identify babies who may experience difficulties with learning from language and help them overcome those difficulties. In the first part of this longitudinal study study (at 14-16 months), we test babies' ability to imagine and name a thing they have never seen. We also measure their memory and attention. When babies are 22-24 months, we repeat the same tests and also ask them to learn new facts about a creature they have never seen. We then test if they can recognize that creature on a picture.This study involves two sessions, one when your baby is between 14 and 16 months old, and the second when your baby is between 22 and 24 months old. During the first session, your baby will watch a video of a person showing objects and naming them. Some objects and names will be familiar and some will be new. In the second session, your baby will watch the same videos and also some new ones in which a person teaches them new facts about a creature they have never seen.

name: Let's go to Sunny School! · Children Helping Science
description: Gender and race gaps are pervasive in the US workforce, especially in fields like STEM. Many efforts to rectify these gaps can actually backfire by making candidates from marginalized backgrounds appear less competent. Here, we test whether preferences for marginalized vs. advantaged candidates are early-emerging. This is important: Studying the development of thinking about marginalized candidates might reveal early cognitive biases that shape perceptions of fairness, merit, and inclusion. Your child will hear a story about Sunny School, a school on a faraway planet. Your child will hear about how the two types of students at school are treated differently. They will watch a teacher select one student to be "class president". Afterwards, your child will answer questions about the chosen class president and why they think the student was chosen.

name: Let your baby show you the way! · Children Helping Science
description: The study explores how babies learn to navigate their visual world. For example, do babies know the which way it would be possible to walk through a cluttered, real-world scene? And do babies learn this difference only once they’ve started walking around themselves? Answering these questions could help us determine how much knowledge babies are born with, and how much they learn through experience. During this study, your child will watch a short video. The video will show short clips of walking through real places. Your child can sit on your lap or in a high chair, and we will record where your child looks as they watch the video. Your job will simply be to remain neutral and supervise.

name: Listen To My Song! · Children Helping Science
description: Previous research has found that babies have various preferences for music - for example, they like to listen to songs that they already know. But different people might sing the same song in a very different way. We still don’t know much about how specific features of a sung melody - such as the pleasantness of a particular person’s voice - might influence infants’ preferences. In this study, we focus on how infants react when different adults sing two well-known songs, “Twinkle, Twinkle, Little Star” and “The Itsy Bitsy Spider.” By observing where your baby looks, their facial expressions, and their movements while listening to these brief clips, we hope to better understand which aspects of singing are most interesting and engaging for infants. In this study, your baby will listen to brief audio clips of adults singing “Twinkle, Twinkle, Little Star” and “The Itsy Bitsy Spider.” While they listen, we will record their reactions, including where they look, their facial expressions, and movements. We will ask you to complete a short survey at the end including demographic questions and questions about your baby's musical experiences. Your baby will hear 16 total songs.

name: Making judgments about robots and people · Children Helping Science
description: Children readily form impressions of others, which affects who they trust, who they turn to with their questions, and who they view as knowledgeable. The purpose of this study is to understand the characteristics that inform children's impressions of others. Past research has shown that children pick up on a variety of different qualities that, in some situations, conflict with each other: confidence and expertise, as well as kindness and benevolence. Are there certain contexts in which children value one social domain over the other? Does this evaluation change over development? Does it differ depending on whether others are humans or robots? This research will provide a greater understanding of what children value in social partners, such as teachers, peers, and parents, which informs effective communication styles in educational and social contexts, especially in the context of emerging technologies such as AI robots. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you Children who participate in this study will listen to characters answer questions about a picture or a word, and then will be asked to give their opinions of those characters (e.g., who they like more; who is nicer; who is smarter). They will also answer some basic questions about themselves, such as how often they change their mind about things or how likely they are to listen to others' opinions.

name: Math Curiosity · Children Helping Science
description: We are interested in investigating the role of math curiosity in learning, how it helps children understand math concepts, and whether it is associated with math knowledge. Specifically, we explore how often children seek feedback and how long they persist when they answer a math problem. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. Once you have signed up, we will email you a link to the electronic parental consent form and a brief 10-item math survey (e.g., 'My child likes learning new things in math'). Please ensure both the consent form and survey are completed prior to your scheduled session.

name: Meet the animals! · Children Helping Science
description: We are interested in how well babies remember which objects they have seen, and whether they remember better if the object (like a particular dog) was given its own name. Babies pay close attention to what we say to them even before they can produce their own words, and this tells us that how we name objects for them can already guide how they think about these objects. In this study, your baby will watch a short video filled with cute, stuffed animals and hear names for these animals (like "Dax"). Your baby will later see these named animals next to new animals that haven't been named. We will then check which animal your baby chooses to look at more. Lastly, your baby will watch short videos testing their memory of colorful shapes.

name: Momo's day at the aquarium · Children Helping Science
description: An exact understanding of number is something that takes children many years to fully acquire. One step along the way is to understand how things can match exactly. Previous research using matching games found that children often have a hard time matching quantities exactly. But the matching games in these studies might not be quite natural and intuitive to very young children. Here we ask whether children demonstrate intuitive understanding of exact quantities more easily in a drawing game. We also ask how children’s quantity matching abilities relate to their counting skills, as measured in the counting game. This study will help us understand how children understand exact equality in different contexts, and will more generally help us better understand the emergence and development of mathematical concepts. In this study your child will draw some pictures and play some games using the keyboard. You will need 10 sheets of plain paper, a dark marker, and a towel to cover the touchpad (if you are using a laptop). Your child will meet Momo, who is an Alien visiting an aquarium. Your child will draw 7 pictures of animals that Momo sees (the extra sheets are in case they want to start over). Your child will also play a matching game where they try to get exactly one present for each family member Momo has, and a counting game where they help Momo buy the right number of different kinds of toys to fit in the spaceship.

name: More Food or Better Food? · Children Helping Science
description: This study examines how children aged 4-9 years old understand and learn about the social world. This research specifically explores children’s understanding of how we give away different items, and how this impacts the assumptions that children make about the relationships of others. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher. During the study, your child will be asked to answer several questions after listening to a series of eight stories with accompanying animations about three people (per story). After each story, your child will be asked to answer several questions, such as how they evaluate resource distribution and differences in resources available.

name: Now you see it! Now you don't! · Children Helping Science
description: We are interested in what babies remember about objects they have seen, and whether they remember it better if the object (like a particular bunny) was given its own name. Babies pay close attention to what we say to them, even before they can produce their own words. This tells us that how we name objects for them can already guide how they think about these objects. Here, we are asking if babies still remember an object even if it is hidden for a moment and then reappears. Your baby’s interest in the object is influenced by the way it was named. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. In this study, you will first fill out a short survey about you and your baby, such as demographics and your child's language experience. Then, your baby will watch a short video filled with cute toys and hear new names for them (like "dax"). Then, your baby will watch as the toy is hidden inside a box, and will then see us retrieve it from the box. We will measure how long your baby looks at the toy that comes out of the box.

name: One or More? · Children Helping Science
description: We know that most adults comfortably use the word "they" to refer to a single person, especially when we don't know the person's gender. For example, most adults would agree that the sentence, "A doctor knows that they should be kind," sounds perfectly fine. What we don't know is when children learn this specific rule. Children first learn "they" as a plural word (as in, "The kids are here. They are playing"). Our goal is to understand when they learn to be flexible and use "they" for a single person, like adults do. Your child will hear short sentences about different things that children might do. They will see pictures that can go with the sentence and choose the picture they think matches best with the sentence. We are interested in which picture children pick when the pronoun in the sentence is singular or plural.

name: Para quem o menino está apontando? · Children Helping Science
description: (Propósito) Muitas das perguntas que seu filho ou filha ouvirão contêm os pronomes “ele/ela”: neste estudo estamos interessados na interpretação que seu filho ou sua filha têm desses pronomes. A interpretação das crianças pode por vezes diferir da dos adultos e queremos compreender porque é que isso acontece e em que idade essas diferenças desaparecem. Neste estudo, seu filho ou sua filha verão figuras com dois personagens, e um dos personagens estará fazendo alguma coisa. Por exemplo, uma imagem poderia ser a de um menino apontando para o avô. Então, seu filho ou sua filha ouvirão uma descrição da imagem, e decidirão se essa descrição está correta ou não.

name: Peek-a-boo, who are you? · Children Helping Science
description: Previous research shows that babies can tell apart kinds of faces they see frequently, but may group less familiar faces together. In this study, we are exploring whether babies notice differences between less familiar kinds of faces or see group them as the same. Your baby will view a series of faces, and we will measure their looking time to understand how they respond. In this study, your baby will see faces of several different women. There will be a total of 20 different women’s faces on screen for several seconds each, and sometimes the same face will appear multiple times while we record how long your baby looks at the screen.

name: Peek & Seek! · Children Helping Science
description: This study will tell us if hearing voice cues (such as “Look, a dog!”) help babies recognize partly covered pictures. We will compare where babies look when they hear speech to where they look when they hear a chiming noise. By measuring the time babies spend looking at the covered or the scrambled picture we can tell if they have “spotted” the picture. Ultimately, this study will help us learn how babies who are just starting to learn language might use the words they hear to better understand different objects in their visual world! During this study your baby will be shown image pairs. One side will have a partly covered version of a picture and the other side will have a scrambled up version of the picture. The pictures will appear for 5 seconds each. Before each picture, there will be one of two audio cues: one will be a voice cue that matches the picture, the other will be a fun chiming noise. We will time your child's eye movements while they listen to the sound and look at the pictures!

name: Probabilistic Learning Across Development: What Will Wiggle Do Next? · Children Helping Science
description: We're studying how children learn to predict others' actions based on their emotions. In particular, we're interested in whether labeling emotions for children helps them make these predictions. If so, this could inform the strategies we use to teach kids about emotions! In this study, your child will play an emotion prediction game with Wiggle, a friendly alien from another planet. Your child will look at pictures of Wiggle's feelings and guess what he will do next.

name: Re-engagement Study for Puppies · Children Helping Science
description: We are investigating whether puppies re-engage their owner with communicative behaviors when their owner stops participating in a cooperative activity, such as playing with them. There is limited information on what puppies understand about participation in joint activities, and this study hopes to help address this. After completing the consent form, you will be redirected to our lab's website where you will find the instructions on how to complete the study on your own without a researcher present. For the procedure, you will be asked to play with your puppy for a minute, then asked to ignore your puppy for a minute, and then repeat this, with a play period at the end. Then, you will be asked to share your video by using a special upload link or a cloud service of your choice. You will be asked to complete the procedure once a month until your puppy reaches 12 months of age.

name: Ready, Set, Think! · Children Helping Science
description: Researchers know that skills like memory, attention, and self-control (called executive functions) develop quickly during early childhood. However, we are still working to understand how to measure these skills accurately, especially in online settings.In this study, we are testing whether simple activities that involve following rules and responding to pictures—like the ones your child will complete—can be used as reliable tools for measuring these abilities. This will help researchers design better studies and improve how we understand early cognitive development. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. During the session, your child will complete six short activities. For example, in one activity your child might be asked to say a different word than what they see on the screen (like saying “night” when shown a picture of the sun), and in another activity they may need to remember and follow simple instructions. At the end of the session, parents will answer a brief set of questions about their child and family, such as age, music exposure, parent education, and general background.

name: Road Trip with Friends! 4.0 · Children Helping Science
description: Children’s interests impact their engagement and performance. Our study asks how children’s intuitive sense of number (their guesses in the first game) relates to their interests (their responses in the second game). Findings from this study will help us better understand what impacts young children’s interests. Ultimately by studying children’s interests, we aim to understand how to help children develop and maintain their interests. In this study, your child will go on a virtual road trip and help some friends along the way! They will play a guessing game to figure out who has the larger amount, then help some friends in a matching game, and then answer some questions about their thoughts on different activities. We are interested to see how children's guesses in the first game relate to their responses in the second game.

name: Science Story Detectives · Children Helping Science
description: We are examining how children of different ages reason about contagious illness. Although young children have some knowledge of risk behaviors and contaminants like germs, children do not usually demonstrate a complete causal understanding of illness transmission until middle childhood. This study will allow us to identify when children begin to causally reason about concepts related to duration, proximity, and contact, and when they can apply this reasoning to a more difficult concept like illness transmission. By identifying the age that children develop general causal reasoning skills needed to understand contagion, it will help us target successful educational interventions for future research. In this study, your child will watch and listen to animated stories about kids getting sick and answer questions about the stories. Each story will describe how several children may or may not have been exposed to germs, and your child will be asked to decide who was most likely to get sick as a result.

name: See The Dots · Children Helping Science
description: Even before babies can talk, their eyes tell us a lot about how they understand the world. Previous research shows that infants will look longer at things that are interesting or meaningful to them. Babies can notice when dots move together in the same direction or when dots are arranged into an organized pattern. However, we still don’t know whether babies notice organized motion sooner than they notice organized shapes. Our study asks two main questions: 1) Do babies prefer looking at dots that move together? 2) Do babies prefer looking at dots that form an overall pattern (form)? By comparing how long babies look at each type of display, we can learn how early visual systems develop and which parts of vision mature first. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. During the session, your child will first watch a short animation of cute fish and then see simple videos made of moving dots; in some, the dots move together or form an overall pattern, and in others they are more random. We are interested in what babies choose to look at, which helps us learn how they use motion and overall pattern to make sense of what they see.
credits: Source: Children Helping Science — public study registry hosted at https://childrenhelpingscience.com. Run by Lourenco Lab (Emory University). name: Sharing and Caring · Children Helping Science
description: Past research shows that children are more likely to share with their friends than with strangers. However, even when we like someone, we might still be unwilling to share with them because the possession is valuable to us. Using short storybooks, the present study asks whether children consider relationships and the value of objects when reasoning about sharing decisions. This study will help us better understand how children make difficult social decisions and decide when to behave prosocially with others. This study will take place on a Zoom video call with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works best for you. A researcher will read your child a few short stories. The stories will be about situations where a character must decide whether or not to share one of their toys with someone else. In some stories, your child will be asked whether they think the character will share their toy. In other stories, your child will be asked why they think the character ultimately shared or did not share their toy.

name: Sharing and connecting · Children Helping Science
description: We are interested in how babies expect things will be shared with others. Previous research has demonstrated that early on in life babies and toddlers expect that things such as items of food will be shared equally and fairly. For example, they might expect that a person would share four crackers equally between two people by giving them both two crackers instead of giving one three and the other person one cracker. We are interested in seeing if babies expect sharers to have positive or negative relationships with the receivers depending on whether they got more or less crackers. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. One of our researchers will show your baby several short videos of a person giving food items to others in a scheduled zoom session. They then see the sharer and the receivers behaving positively or negatively. We want to see which events your baby chooses to look at!

name: Should I do what majority wants or what I want? · Children Helping Science
description: Previous research tells us that young children take into account majority groups and align with them promptly over minority groups. However, in this study, we are interested in whether this still stands when we give children a leadership position with the possibility to go against a majority group in order to choose their own interest. Would children prefer to do an activity that the majority wants to do, or an activity that the minority and themselves wants to do? Overall, this study will provide more insight into children’s complex decision making! This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. During the Zoom meeting you will meet one of our researchers and they will tell you a story about students in a classroom who want to do certain activities. In this game, your participating child is the leader of the classroom and they get to decide what the rest of the students will do; however, the classroom is split! Participants need to decide whether they want to go with what the majority of the classroom wants to do, or what they (and other few students) want to do.

name: Social Influence and Popularity · Children Helping Science
description: Social dynamics play a crucial role in how we navigate interactions with others. However, little is known about how infants make sense of social influence. The present study asks how infants respond to individuals (here, animated characters) who are imitated by others. Such acts of imitation may communicate that an individual has social influence over others. This research will shed light on infants' understanding of social influence, as well as how children may begin to reason about leadership and expertise. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Participate Now!” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. In this Zoom-based study, your child will observe animated characters (3D shapes with eyes) either imitate or not imitate other characters.

name: Stories with silly sentences · Children Helping Science
description: This research explores how and when children learn various aspects of their first language, particularly when those aspects vary across languages. For example, languages can have different word orders ("the red dog" or "the dog red"), or different ways of expressing when something has happened (past)/will happen (future). By looking at these differences, this study aims to discover what children have in common as they learn about their language. In this study, your child will listen to some stories which sometimes have silly sentences. We want to know what children think about these silly sentences, based on what they are looking at. We will ask you to wear headphones while your child listens to the sentences, so that you do not hear the sentences (and react differently to the silly ones yourself).

name: Storytelling in Spanish and English · Children Helping Science
description: The goal of this study is to learn more about how mothers use both Spanish and English when talking to their babies, including how they produce words and sounds in different languages, and how the way they talk to their babies compares to the way they talk to adults. Previous studies have compared speech directed to an infant to speech directed to an adult in several different languages, but haven't often looked at what happens when parents are switching between two languages, which we know happens often in bilingual families! Ultimately, knowing more about the speech that babies are exposed to when they are acquiring language will help us to figure out how it is that they are able to learn two languages at the same time. This is a study for Spanish-English bilingual mothers to participate in with their babies! Over several sessions that you record and send to us, you will look at pictures and tell stories about them in both languages. You will tell three stories to your baby, and three stories to an adult. Each time, you will tell one story using just English, one story using just Spanish, and one story when you switch between the two languages whenever you want. We will send you all of the pictures, and you will record yourself telling the stories at whatever time works best for your family. First you will fill out a short survey to make sure that you and your baby are both the correct age for this study and speak the correct languages. Then, we will email you more information including a way for you to provide informed consent. Finally, you will receive pdf copies of six picture book, instructions for recording yourself telling stories about those pictures and submitting those recordings, and a link to a questionnaire about how you typically use both languages.

name: Super Surprise! · Children Helping Science
description: We know that babies expect objects to adhere to gravity and friends to be happy for each other when they succeed. In these kinds of studies, babies look longer at events that defy their expectations, but we don't know whether babies look equally long at events that feature different kinds of surprises. In this study, we ask whether babies are more surprised by one kind of unexpected event compared to the other, or if babies are equally surprised by different unexpected events. This would tell us whether babies are sensitive to surprises in the world, regardless of what kind they are, or whether some babies are more attuned to object surprises while others are more attuned to social surprises. This is especially important because infants also learn from surprising events, so knowing if babies are better detectors of one kind of surprise versus the other could tell us what might help them learn better in each situation! This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. Babies look longer at events that are surprising to them, but are they equally surprised by objects that do surprising things as they are characters who do surprising things? First, your baby will see two objects. One object will behave expectedly, while the other will behave unexpectedly. Then your baby will watch one character be happy for their friend's success and then be sad for their friend's success later on. We measure how long your baby looks at all of the events, and ask whether your baby is more surprised by one set of events over the other.

name: Symbol Learning · Children Helping Science
description: This study investigates babies’ ability to learn different rules, and whether some rules are easier to learn than others. For example, if we first show babies that a star means they should look to a green box, is it easier to learn that a new symbol means they should look to the "not green" box when we combine the familiar symbol with another symbol (like a star surrounded by circles) than when we show them a completely new symbol (like a diamond)? Answers to these questions will help us learn how children might learn language using similar rules. During the study, your baby will sit on your lap or a high chair and watch short movies with sounds. Your baby will see movies teaching them different rules about different symbols and shapes. For example, they might see a video teaching them that when they see a star, they should look to the green box.

name: Tag Team with Gizmo! · Children Helping Science
description: The goal of this study is to understand how children navigate interpersonal challenges when working with a teammate, such as keeping a teammate engaged and motivated when a task becomes frustrating. Specifically, prior research has shown that children can assign their teammates specific tasks based on how well they perform in a certain task. However, we don't yet know if children can make these assignments based on the emotions (e.g., frustration) of their teammates, not just their competence. Understanding how children navigate the emotional challenges of collaborations – and where they struggle – will help us learn how children build lifelong social skills, and has important implications for the science of teamwork. Children will work together to count stars in the galaxy with Gizmo, a friendly alien from outer space. Children will look at various pictures of stars in the galaxy and tell us (i) if they saw more stars of a certain color, (ii) how challenging they felt that choice to be, and (iii) whether the next picture should go to themselves, or to their teammate, Gizmo. Occasionally, Gizmo might struggle with the star-counting game and display some negative emotions.

name: The costs of social actions · Children Helping Science
description: We all know that toys, snacks, and video games cost money. But did you know that our behavior can have a price tag too? We’re talking about “social costs”, meaning the downsides that we sometimes experience when interacting with our peers. In this study, your child will help us understand many of the important social tradeoffs that every kid faces. In these tricky situations, we are interested in what children think people will do, what people will think, and how they will feel. To get a full look at children’s social worlds, we go both inside and outside the classroom, capturing many settings where children interact. At school, we look at how children think about the advantages and disadvantages of popularity, how they think people will feel when their friends don’t tell them personal news right away, and how they think about assigning credit or giving feedback for different outcomes. We also ask what children think about the choice to explain certain things to others. On the playground we look at how making new friends can be fun, but it makes old friends feel left out. By asking about these different contexts, we hope to capture how children have strong ideas about some of these tradeoffs and may be less sure of others. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. One of our researchers will read stories aloud to your child during the scheduled Zoom session. The stories will be about different friends going through conflicts. Then, the researcher will ask what your child thinks the friends will do, how they will feel, and why your child thinks that.

name: There was a misunderstanding! What will happen Next? · Children Helping Science
description: In this study, we are exploring how toddlers understand when communication goes smoothly and when it breaks down. Previous research shows that even very young children can notice when someone misunderstands a message. In this study, we’re testing whether toddlers can also predict how people will respond in these moments: for example, whether or not someone will fulfill a request after they indicate confusion by saying “What?”. By seeing how toddlers understand the communicative meaning of the word "what?" and can use it to manage their expectations, we can better understand how they develop early communication and social reasoning skills. In this study, your child will watch short video clips showing two people having a conversation. Sometimes a person will ask for an object, and someone will respond with "Yeah!". Sometimes, one person misunderstands what the other said and says, “What?” and looks confused. Your child will be asked what they think will happen next (for example, which object will someone pick based on the person's responses to the request). After your child finishes watching the videos, you’ll be asked to complete a few short surveys about your child’s language development and home environment. These help us understand how communication skills grow across children of different ages.
credits: Source: Children Helping Science — public study registry hosted at https://childrenhelpingscience.com. Run by Bergelson Lab at Harvard (Harvard University). Participation requires a Children Helping Science family account; running the study then happens on the lab's own platform (Lookit, Zoom, Qualtrics, etc.). name: Think About Teachers + Play a Guessing Game! · Children Helping Science
description: We’re interested in how children handle uncertainty when they have to make decisions without being completely sure of the answer. Differences in how sensitive children are to uncertainty may also be related to other parts of their decision-making and social reasoning, including how they learn from other people. This study has two goals. First, we want to find out whether children prefer to be very sure before making a choice or whether they feel comfortable deciding when they don’t have all the information. Second, we want to understand how these differences connect to the ways children seek out information from others. Your child will play two different games today. In the first game, your child will watch short videos of four different teachers. The first two teachers will teach their students the names of their toys. The last two teachers will teach their students about how they could make their projects better. After watching each video, your child will answer some questions to help us understand how teachers' feedback influences what children think about them. In the second game, we will show your child a pair of boxes that are filled with different colored balls. Then, one of these boxes will be hidden behind a curtain. Your child will use a claw machine to take balls out of the box in order to figure out which box is behind the curtain! After showing your child how to use the claw machine and play the guessing game, your child will see 4 pairs of boxes in total. NOTE: This study is only compatible with laptop / desktop computers, and not with phones / tablets.

Participation requires a Children Helping Science family account; running the study then happens on the lab's own platform (Lookit, Zoom, Qualtrics, etc.). Metadata reproduced for non-commercial cataloging only — consult the lab and the linked landing page for licence and consent terms.

name: Thinking about preferences and rules · Children Helping Science
description: The purpose of this study is to help us better understand the kinds of situations children can think and talk about. We present children with short stories which use counterfactual conditionals - a type of sentence that lets us talk about events which are not real. For example, “If Giraffes had wings, they would fly” is a counterfactual, because it tells us something about the state of the world if Giraffes had wings (even though they don’t in real life!). Through this study we want to analyze whether children respond more or less like adults when thinking about counterfactuals involving mental state variables (e.g., someone's beliefs, someone's adherence to rules), and whether different kinds of mental state variables are easier for children to think about counterfactually. We test whether external factors (social norms and rules, e.g., a child having to finish their homework before they can play with their friends) have the same or a different influence than internal factors (preferences and opinions, e.g., liking certain toppings on pizza) on a child’s ability to reason counterfactually. In doing so we gain a deeper understanding of the kinds of situations children area able to reason over. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. In this study, your child will watch a series of short videos and answer questions related to these videos. Your child will listen to four short stories, and based on their understanding of the stories, will be prompted to answer questions about what they think would have happened if elements of those stories had been changed. For example, they will be told about a character's preference for a certain type of pizza (with either pepperoni or mushrooms), and will be asked if the character would still have eaten the pizza if changes were made to it (e.g, no mushrooms, no pepperoni).

name: Thinking About "What-If" · Children Helping Science
description: We are interested in understanding the unique approach school-age children take when thinking about things that could have happened, but didn’t. We aim to explore how imagination shapes the way that children reason about hypothetical scenarios. This study will help us better understand the developmental links between two seemingly distinct, but important cognitive skills: imagination and reasoning. Your child will begin with a short example story to learn how the study works. Then, they'll watch and listen to two animated stories about spending a fun day with a friend (e.g., at the beach). In each story, something unexpected happens that causes a problem. Your child will then be asked to answer some questions about the stories. At the end, they'll play a few short game-like activities that involve remembering things, paying attention, and following rules.

name: Tick-Tock, Baby Clock · Children Helping Science
description: Babies use many kinds of information—such as size, number, and how long something takes—to make sense of everyday events. Previous research suggests that infants can connect some of these cues, like matching a longer line with a longer sound. This study builds on that work by asking whether babies use how long a pouring event takes to form expectations about how much water they should see afterward. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. You may participate in this study twice. The second session should be scheduled no sooner than 2 days and no later than 3 weeks after the first session. During the session, your baby will watch videos of water being poured into a container. After each pouring video, your baby will see two outcomes: one with an amount of water that matches the length of the pour, and one that is impossible because it is either too much or too little for that pour. Babies often show what they find surprising by looking longer. Therefore, we will observe how long your baby looks at each outcome to see whether they look longer at the impossible one, indicating that this outcome violates their expectation.

Listed at https://childrenhelpingscience.com/studies/2eb470c2-f986-4324-8d04-6029e62644a2/. name: Toy Showing Study for Adult Dogs · Children Helping Science
description: We are investigating whether dogs will show a new toy to their owner who has not seen the toy. The aim is to determine whether dogs 1) show their new toy to their owner simply to share attention to it with them or 2) give their new toy to their owner to get them to play with it. The study procedure is composed of two parts: Part 1 is done over 2 days for approximately 10 minutes each day, and Part 2 is done over 2 days for approximately 15 minutes each day. For Part 1 of the study, you will be asked to play with your dog for 5 minutes an hour before the second adult is expected to arrive home, and then record your dog when the second adult arrives home. For Part 2 of the study, you will be asked to repeat the same procedure, except this time you will also be asked to play with your dog using a new toy. After completing the consent form, you will be redirected to our lab's website where you will find the instructions on how to complete the study on your own without a researcher present.
credits: Source: Children Helping Science — public study registry hosted at https://childrenhelpingscience.com. Run by Pets Assisting With Science (PAWS) Lab (University of St Andrews). Listed at https://childrenhelpingscience.com/studies/bfefd9d1-af1c-4365-9ad0-5fd8ecc52a7c/. Metadata reproduced for non-commercial cataloging only — consult the lab and the linked landing page for licence and consent terms.

name: What can you remember? Memory of Numbers, Pictures, and Stories · Children Helping Science
description: Children use their expectations about the world to guide their exploration and learning. Notably, in instances where event outcomes are unexpected, children tend to pay more attention and, in some instances, have better memory for it. However, it remains unclear whether different levels of unexpectedness or surprise impact children's memory and learning differently. This study explores how different event outcomes impact children’s memory and learning of storybook content. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. In this study, we are interested in how children remember the details of stories. Your child will play 3 memory games with a researcher over Zoom—a counting game, a picture game, and a storybook game. In the counting game, your child will hear and recite a series of numbers (e.g., 123). In the picture game, children will see and label pictures of a sun and a moon as either “night” or “day”. Finally, in the storybook game, children will listen to 6 ebook stories about different cartoon characters. Each storybook will have a different surprise ending (e.g., not surprising, a little surprising, or very surprising). Then they will answer multiple choice questions about each story assessing their memory for storybook event details (e.g., who, what, where and why).
credits: Source: Children Helping Science — public study registry hosted at https://childrenhelpingscience.com. Run by RUN Child Development Lab (Rutgers University - Newark). Listed at https://childrenhelpingscience.com/studies/f00ae309-71cf-4b10-a9ea-eee4fc68b4cb/. Participation requires a Children Helping Science family account; running the study then happens on the lab's own platform (Lookit, Zoom, Qualtrics, etc.). name: What do you and your partner think about raising a child with autism? · Children Helping Science
description: We are interested in understanding the differences between co-parents mental health and attitudes surrounding their child's autism. This study will assess the degree to which parental attitude similarity will predict caregiver mental health, and whether social support plays a role in this relationship. We are also interested in characterizing the experiences of fathers of children with autism as they are significantly underrepresented in existing autism research. Participation involves completing an online survey on Qualtrics that takes around twenty minutes to complete. One co-parent will complete a series of questionnaires including demographics about you and your child, an autism severity scale, scales measuring attitudes towards neurodiversity, measures of mental health functioning (including stress, anxiety, and feelings of burden), and finally a measure of social support. Participants are then encouraged to share the survey with their partner. Then the other co-parent will complete the same survey.

name: What Do You Know About Emotions in Others? · Children Helping Science
description: In this study, we are exploring how different ways of explaining emotions help children develop empathy and reasoning skills. Research has shown that causal reasoning helps children to consider the influence of actions and events on emotions and prosocial behaviors, but little is known about how children build these causal reasoning skills. One mechanism we believe to be important in the development of causal reasoning and empathy is the use of causal language in child-directed speech. We are specifically interested in how "causal talk" (explaining why someone feels a certain way) influences a child's ability to recognize and share the feelings of others. Your participation will help us learn more about the developmental building blocks of emotional intelligence. By observing how children respond to different types of stories, we can better understand how caregivers and educators can support healthy social-emotional growth in early childhood. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. Your child will participate in a series of storytelling activities where they hear short descriptions of different characters' experiences in everyday situations and will be asked to share their thoughts on how some of those characters might be feeling. We will vary the amount of causal language in the descriptions that the children hear to see if that changes children's responses! Parents will be asked to complete a brief empathy-related questionnaire (27 items) where they will be asked to state how much they identify with certain statements (e.g., "Watching a happy movie makes me happy") and provide basic demographic and language background information.

name: What is a good explanation? · Children Helping Science
description: There has been conflicting research regarding the type of explanations as well as how much detail within explanations children prefer and learn from. Here, in this study, we systematically examine the level of detail within different types of explanations that satisfy children's curiosity and aid in their learning. By determining the optimal explanation, we can help parents and educators understand which types of explanations foster better understanding and exploration in young children. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. Your child and the experimenter will be reading a story together and answering questions about new and fun characters from a distant planet! Your child will be helping decide what the characters are doing and generating explanations for their behaviors.

name: What is that toy called? · Children Helping Science
description: This study will help us learn if children prioritize learning from someone who is accurate over someone who is inaccurate. In this study, children will have the opportunity to learn from either a person or a robot. Prior research has found that older children will learn from a robot who is accurate over a person who is inaccurate in a word-learning task. Here we are testing the same thing but using gestures, pointing, rather than word-learning. This will help us understand what qualities a teacher needs to have to be effective and engaging to young children. In this study, your child will watch videos of a person and a robot labelling toys. Then, your child will answer questions and play a matching game! In the matching game, our child will be shown pictures of a robot and a person and be asked whether something mechanical (gears) or something biological (a heart) belongs inside. Finally, children will be read statements about different people and be asked which person they would like to be friends with.

name: What's counting for babies? 4.0 · Children Helping Science
description: Babies hear count words as early as a few months of age. But do babies know that counting is about quantity? And if so, what about counting (e.g., does it have to be "one, two, three"?) draws babies' attention to quantity? Your baby's reaction to our "counting" videos will tell us what types of activities draw babies’ attention to quantities, and will ultimately help scientists understand where our concept of counting comes from. In this study, your baby will watch different numbers of toys being pointed at and labeled in different ways (such as "one, two, three" or "two, three, one"). We are interested in how babies react to different types of activities that resemble counting. This is a new version of "What's counting for babies" - if eligible, your child can participate even if you've done a previous version of this study! You will need a high chair or stroller for this study.

name: What's My Friend Doing? · Children Helping Science
description: This study will allow us to examine how well children learn new verbs based on watching first-person clips of a child their own age performing an action. Past studies have shown how children can learn through observation, but have primarily focused on learning from third-person and adult viewpoints. This study tests whether children can learn new verbs from a perspective that resembles theirs. This study is also being conducted in the Netherlands, allowing researchers to learn more about how kids from differing cultures acquire verbs for everyday actions. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. Your child will watch an interactive video in which they see a child performing an action labeled with a made-up verb (e.g., “She is going to tam it”). After watching the video, participants will see a split screen showing two different actions being performed. The research assistant will then ask the child to point to or say aloud which action matches with the made-up verb.

name: What's That Sound? · Children Helping Science
description: How do children combine what they see and what they hear to figure out what happened when they didn't see what happened? Earlier research found that children do not fully develop the ability to combine visual and sound information until around age eight. But those earlier studies used a game where balls dropped into a box, and younger children were often distracted by thinking that balls should fall straight down, like how gravity works. This made it hard to tell whether children could imagine what was happening. Our new study avoids that problem by showing videos where the ball is moving sideways instead of falling. This makes it easier for younger children to mentally simulate the ball’s movement and use both what they see and what they hear to infer who rolled it. In this study, your child will watch short videos of two characters rolling a ball back and forth behind a screen. They will hear a sound and use it to guess which character rolled the ball.

name: Wheel of Fortune · Children Helping Science
description: We are interested in studying how feelings of control relate to different ways of coping, such as regulating our emotions or tackling a problem. By seeing how your child responds in our tasks, we can learn more about how children develop ways to cope and how this might change as a function of how in control they feel. During the study, your child will play a game that involves stopping a spinning wheel at the right moment. In between the spins, we will ask your child a few questions: how much control they felt they had, how difficult the game was, whether it felt stressful, whether they tried to change how they felt, and whether they thought it was worth putting effort into the game. At the end, your child will answer 42 questions about how often they feel different things. Some of these are negative experiences, for example, how often they think about death or if they feel worthless.

name: Which block structure is harder to build? · Children Helping Science
description: The ability to reason about the difficulty of a task at hand is crucial to learning. For example, it helps us decide which goals to choose for ourselves and others, how to allocate our time and resources, and how to avoid unecessary failure. But how do we know how hard a given task is when we have not engaged in it before? In this study, we are interested in how children judge how easy (or hard) it is to complete different novel blockbuilding structures. The study will help us understand how children gauge task difficulty. Your child will see pictures of people playing with blocks, and they will judge how hard or easy it is to build various block structures!

name: Which character pronounced the word that way? · Children Helping Science
description: The aim of this study is to explore the social information (e.g., race and sex/gender of the speaker) that children track during language processing. Our approach to studying language acquisition not only considers real-world linguistic and social diversity but is sensitive to how this diversity supports language learning. This research will aid psychologists, educators, speech-language practitioners, and policymakers as children in Canada and the United States interact with speakers of diverse racial, linguistic, and cultural backgrounds across various settings. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. During the study, your child will meet with a researcher who will introduce them to clipart characters and novel objects on the screen. They will hear the characters label the objects using different types of pronunciations. During each trial, your child will decide which type of speaker used a specific pronunciation. At the end of the study, we will ask them, “What helped you decide who said each word?”

name: Which Friend Said It Best? · Children Helping Science
description: This study examines how well children understand grammar, presenting them with two sentences, only one of which is grammatically correct. Seeing whether or not they can correctly identify the “better” sentence allows us to better grasp when children begin to understand sentence structure. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. The study will consist of a short video in which the child will be asked to listen to cartoon characters talk. They will be offered two similar sentences and be instructed to either only listen or indicate "which friend said it best" by pointing to the corresponding cartoon person. For example, the cartoons may say "Jessica tore the leaf." and "Jessica tore at the leaf." and the child would be asked to point at the "friend that said it best."

name: Which group should join the Reading Club? · Children Helping Science
description: As adults, we have different opinions about the under-representation of marginalized groups (gender, race) in elite fields. Should we intervene to increase representation (in the name of fairness), or step back (in the name of fairness)? Many factors may impact our answers to these questions. In this study, we are curious about how young children, who are just learning about the world around them, address under-representation. Do they use equity-based (i.e., disadvantaged people should get more) or equality-based (i.e., everyone should get the same) solutions to rectify under-representation? How do their developing political attitudes guide their decisions? Understanding the emergence of equity-based vs. equality-based thinking in this context will inform conversations with parents and educators about equity in the classroom. First, you will complete two questionnaires about your beliefs about social groups. Then, your child will learn about two different groups of students on a faraway planet. They will also learn about a school and a Reading Club on the planet. Your child will count how many students are in the Reading Club. Then, they will decide who gets to join the Reading Club. Finally, they will answer questions about the social hierarchies on the planet, fairness, and conformity.

name: Which thing is Weepo looking for? · Children Helping Science
description: In this study, we’re interested in what young children know about the words we use to describe certain kinds of actions. Some actions are ones we can easily see, like jumping or hugging. Others, like imagining or forgetting or are not readily observable. This second set of action words, or verbs, are harder to learn and take longer to acquire in child development, because they place restrictions on the kind of actions they pick out—and this is the kind of thing we don’t teach children! For example, we don't tell them that their blanket or their sippy cup can't regret or believe something. We are investigating whether two-year-olds have this knowledge, because it helps us understand what young children know about these words when they first acquire them, and what has to be learned later on. This study will take place on a video call, with a live researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar through our lab's Bookings page where you can select a date and time that works for you. During the study, your child will interact with one of our researchers and character on screen who is practicing their words. Your child will see some images, then the character will describe one, and ask your child to find it by pointing to an image.

name: Which Way Should We Go? · Children Helping Science
description: As artificial intelligence (AI) has increasingly become a part of our daily lives, we are interested in how children think, plan, and work together when interacting with AI agents. Prior research shows that young children can collaborate well with peers through mechanisms such as joint commitment, but we know little about how children collaborate with AI agents, which can perform well in individual tasks but may lack the joint commitment mechanisms found in humans. Through interactive computer games, we study how children make social decisions with either peers or virtual AI agents. It helps us understand which aspects of cooperation transfer to AI partners and which may be unique to human-to-human teamwork. This study will take place on a Zoom video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate!” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. In this study, your child will play a short computer game similar to Pac-Man. In the game, your child will guide a dot on the screen to various destinations using the arrow keys. First, your child will complete a few practice rounds where they play a solo game. Once they are familiar with the game, your child will play with another player (another dot) in the game. This other player will either be controlled by another child online or by a bot that is managed by a computer program. In the two-player game, they will collaborate to finish the task. After the games, we will ask them a few short questions about their game experience (e.g., would you play with this teammate again?). Before the session, we will email you a demographic survey with questions, such as age, race/ethnicity, and household income.

name: Which wub knows how to activate the toy? · Children Helping Science
description: As adults, it's often easy for us to reason about what others know. Sometimes this is as easy as listening to what someone says or watching how they behave. However, we can also reason about what others know by observing how they influence others. In this study, children are presented with scenarios where speakers interact with a listener by either speaking gibberish or sneezing towards them. Based on how the listeners act towards a toy afterwards, we're curious if children can reason about whether the speaker themselves was knowledgeable about that toy. Your child will be introduced to friendly creatures - the wubs - and will watch two videos about them. In the videos, the wubs interact with a toy, pressing the buttons and lever, and with each other, either speaking or sneezing. After watching the videos, your child will be asked a question about which of two wubs knows how to operate the toy.

name: Who do I see? · Children Helping Science
description: We are interested in how well babies remember which faces they have seen, and whether they remember better if the face (like a particular individual) was given its own name. Babies pay close attention to what we say to them even before they can produce their own words, and this tells us that how we name objects for them can already guide how they think about these objects. In this study, your baby will watch a short video filled with faces of different individuals and hear names for these faces (like "dax"). Your baby will later see these faces next to new faces that haven't been named. We will then check which face your baby chooses to look at more.

name: Who's a Better Friend? · Children Helping Science
description: This study is about how infants understand and learn about the social relationship. We specifically aim to investigate what influences infants’ understanding of others’ social relationships. Previous research has shown that infants understand that others' antisocial behavior can change their relationships, but it remains unclear how prosocial behavior influences those relationships. This study will contribute to our understanding of how infants perceive the impact of various social behaviors on social relationships. This study will take place on a video call, live with two researchers. This video call will take around 15-30 minutes. Clicking on the “Participate Now!” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. During the study, you will be asked to take a seat on a chair and put your baby on your lap. Your baby will be presented with videos about puppets interacting with one another. They will engage in different types of interactions including prosocial or antisocial behaviors. We will record your baby’s looking patterns to measure their understanding of their social interactions, and more broadly, social relationships. You will be with your baby at all times. You will also be asked to fill out a consent form, that can take 5-10 minutes total, which will be emailed with a link to you before your scheduled time.

name: Who's the Helper? · Children Helping Science
description: In this study, we want to understand if young children can recognize caregiving relationships. Your child will first hear stories in which some characters of similar or different sizes engage in friendly social contact (hugging, hand-holding). We will ask your child questions about who is the helper (who gives food, comforts, or makes someone laugh) based on the relationships between characters in the story! Previous studies suggest that children use cues of closeness to represent relationships. In the present study, we want to know if children use information about the character's relationships and size to guess whether a character will comfort another character in distress. In this scheduled video chat with a researcher, your child will hear stories in which some characters engage in friendly social actions (hugging, hand-holding), and answer questions about who is the helper (who gives food, comforts, or makes someone laugh) based on the relationships between characters in the story. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Participate Now!” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you.

name: Who shares my sweet tooth? · Children Helping Science
description: Previous research shows babies can infer affiliation between two people with shared language and even food. However, whether they expect a social goup to share preferences for the same kind of candy is still an open question. This study will explore whether babies expect members of a group to like the same things. This study will take place on a video call, live with a researcher! Clicking on the “Schedule a time to participate” button will send you to an online calendar where you can select a date and time that works for you. In this Zoom-based study, your baby will watch videos of different people eating candy and reacting to it. We will record where the baby looks the entire time for their reaction while they are seated on the caregiver's lap.

name: Who Should We Invite to Our Playdate? For Children with Autism Spectrum Disorder · Children Helping Science
description: We're studying how children with autism spectrum disorder understand promises and the reasons people give when they break them. During the study, your child watches puppets who promised to show a cool toy but didn't bring one, giving different reasons. We then ask kids whether it was wrong that each puppet didn't bring the toy and why they thought so. We are looking at how autistic children's sense of right and wrong develops. For children with an autism spectrum diagnosis (ASD)! In this study, your child will meet a friendly Head Puppet who introduces other animal puppet friends. Each puppet promises to show your child a cool toy, but sometimes they don't bring one! Your child will be asked to share their thoughts about what happened and which puppets they liked. To participate, you'll schedule a Zoom session with our research team at a time that works for your family.
